detail.question: A risk that has three possible outcomes — loss, gain, or no change — is a:
detail.explanation: Para 1.2: speculative risk involves three possible outcomes: loss, gain, or no change (e.g. buying shares). Insurers will not insure speculative risks. Pure risk has no possibility of gain.

detail.question: Which type of risk is the ONLY kind that can be insured?
options: Any risk with a monetary value
detail.explanation: Para 1.3: pure risk (loss or no loss, no possibility of gain) is the only kind that can be insured, because insurance compensates for loss, not to provide an opportunity for gain.

detail.question: Which is NOT one of the requirements of an insurable risk?
options: The loss must be catastrophic to the insurer
The loss must occur by chance
detail.explanation: Para 2.1: the loss must NOT be catastrophic to the insurer. A catastrophic loss (e.g. nuclear risk) is uninsurable. Watch the word NOT — three options are real requirements; the catastrophic one is reversed.

detail.question: Suicide within a certain period of policy inception is excluded mainly because it fails which requirement of an insurable risk?
options: The loss must occur by chance
The loss must be based on large numbers
detail.explanation: Para 2.5: the loss must occur by chance (be accidental, not intentional). Suicide is not accidental, so it is excluded within a set period of inception.

detail.question: An employer sets aside its own money to pay staff medical expenses instead of buying insurance. This method of dealing with risk is:
detail.explanation: Para 3.5: retaining the risk means assuming full financial responsibility (self-insuring). Transferring the risk is buying insurance; avoiding is not undertaking the activity; controlling is loss prevention.

detail.question: Careful smoking aside — a person who is overweight or has a history of heart attacks presents which kind of hazard?
detail.explanation: Para 7.2: a physical hazard is a physical characteristic that increases the likelihood of loss (e.g. heart-attack history, being overweight). Moral hazard is dishonesty (para 7.3). Note: the M9 syllabus recognises only physical and moral hazards.

detail.question: A person applies for a sum assured far in excess of what others in the same financial situation would buy, possibly for a dishonest reason. This is an example of:
detail.explanation: Para 7.3: moral hazard is the likelihood that a person acts dishonestly in the insurance transaction (e.g. seeking excessive cover, giving false information).

detail.question: Under Section 146 of the Insurance Act, insurable interest for a LIFE insurance policy must exist:
options: At the inception of the contract only
At both inception and the time of the life insured's death
At the time of death only
detail.explanation: Para 14.13: for life insurance, insurable interest must be present at inception, but need NOT be present at the time of the life insured's death. A beneficiary need not prove insurable interest to claim.

detail.question: For most GENERAL insurance policies, insurable interest must exist:
options: At the time of loss only
At both inception and the time of loss
It is not required for general insurance
detail.explanation: Para 14.12: in most general insurance policies, insurable interest must be present at both inception and the time of loss. (Marine cargo is the exception: time of loss only.)

detail.question: Which type of insurance requires insurable interest at the TIME OF LOSS ONLY?
detail.explanation: Para 14.12: marine cargo insurance is the exception — insurable interest need not exist at inception, only at the time of loss. Life is inception only; other general insurances are both.

detail.question: Under Section 146, a person may effect insurance on their child's or ward's life if the child or ward, at the time the policy is effected, is:
detail.explanation: Para 14.17 (s.146(1)(b)(iii)): a person may insure a child or ward under the age of 18 at the time the policy is effected.

detail.question: Which of the following need NOT be disclosed by a proposer under the duty of utmost good faith?
options: The proposer's insurance and claims history
detail.explanation: Para 13.7: facts which lessen the risk need not be disclosed (e.g. avoiding hazardous activities). Criminal convictions, insurance/claims history and occupational hazards ARE material facts. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: The duty of disclosure for a LIFE insurance policy is revived:
options: Only when a claim is made
On alteration, such as increasing the sum assured
On annual renewal, like general insurance
Never, once the policy is issued
detail.explanation: Para 13.12: on alteration (e.g. increasing sum assured), the duty is revived as a new contract is formed. Para 13.11: there is no revival on 'renewal' for life policies, as no new contract is formed.

detail.question: The Law of Large Numbers allows an insurer to:
options: Predict the final cost of claims fairly confidently as the insured group grows
Eliminate all risk of catastrophic loss
detail.explanation: Paras 12.2, 12.5: the greater the number of insured persons, the more the actual loss experience tends toward the expected, letting the insurer predict claims and charge a fixed premium.

detail.question: A key difference between insurance and gambling is that:
options: Insurance offers the buyer a chance of financial gain
Gambling creates a new speculative risk, while insurance handles an existing pure risk
detail.explanation: Para 6.1: gambling creates a new speculative risk, whereas insurance is a technique for handling an existing pure risk. No new risk is created by insurance.

detail.question: When an insurer's actuary calculates premiums to cover only the expected cost of claims, this is the:
detail.explanation: Para 1.12-1.13: the net premium covers the cost of claims; the loading (for expenses and profit margin) is then added to give the gross premium.

detail.question: The amount added to the net premium to cover the insurer's expenses and profit margin is called the:
detail.explanation: Para 1.13: the total added to the net premium to cover expenses and provide a profit margin is the loading; net premium + loading = gross premium.

detail.question: All else being equal, a single premium is likely to be LOWER than the sum of all future regular premiums because:
options: The insurer has the full amount upfront to invest, earning higher returns
Single premium policies carry no expenses
detail.explanation: Para (b) Single Premiums: the insurer has the full premium upfront for investment, earning higher returns, so the single premium tends to be lower than the sum of future regular premiums.

detail.question: A policy where the owner makes single premium payments on a regular basis but can stop future payments anytime, with the policy treated as fully paid-up, is a:
detail.explanation: Para (c) Recurrent Single Premiums: like regular premiums, payments are made on a regular basis, but the owner can discontinue future payments without affecting the policy (treated as fully paid-up). One payment done forever = single; a stoppable series = recurrent single.

detail.question: Under current CPF rules, which premium types MAY be used to buy a policy with CPF monies?
options: Single premium or recurrent single premium
detail.explanation: Para (c): under current CPF rules, only Single Premium or Recurrent Single Premium policies may be purchased using CPF monies (other restrictions apply).

detail.question: A limited premium whole life policy, compared with an ordinary (regular premium) whole life policy of the same sum assured, has:
options: Lower premiums over a longer period
Higher premiums over a shorter period
detail.explanation: Para (e) Limited Premium: premiums are paid for a specified period only, and are higher than a normal regular premium policy because the same cost is spread over a shorter period.

detail.question: Paying premiums monthly instead of annually usually costs more in total because the insurer:
options: Loses interest it could have earned and incurs extra processing expenses
detail.explanation: Para (a) Regular Premiums: frequent payments cost the insurer lost interest and extra processing, so these are costed in — e.g. some insurers charge an extra 2% of annual premium for monthly payment.

detail.question: Participating (with-profits) policies are life insurance products that:
options: Share in the profits or surplus of the life insurance fund via bonuses/dividends
Track the performance of investment units
Guarantee a fixed return with no bonuses
detail.explanation: Para 2.4: participating policies share in the profits or surplus of the fund; owners receive bonuses and/or dividends as declared. Non-par policies do not share in surplus.

detail.question: Which of the following can ONLY be a non-participating policy?
detail.explanation: Para 2.5: non-participating examples are Universal Life and Term Insurance. Note after 2.5: Whole Life and Endowment are offered in BOTH par and non-par forms. So Term is non-par only.

detail.question: Which policy types are offered in BOTH participating and non-participating forms?
detail.explanation: Note after para 2.5: Whole Life and Endowment Insurance policies are offered in both participating and non-participating forms. Term and Universal Life are non-par only.

detail.question: Investment-linked policies differ from traditional products because they:
options: Combine insurance protection with investment in funds, with values tracking the units
Are pure protection with no investment
Guarantee both death benefit and cash value
Pay bonuses from the participating fund
detail.explanation: Paras 2.6-2.7: ILPs combine insurance protection with investment in funds managed by insurers or third parties; assets and liabilities of the funds are kept separate.

detail.question: A First-to-die life insurance policy pays the death benefit:
options: In equal shares to both lives
To the estate regardless of who dies
On the death of the first of the lives insured, then cover ends
Only when both lives insured have died
detail.explanation: Table 3.1: a first-to-die policy pays on the death of one of the lives insured, to the surviving life insured, and the cover then ends. Last survivor (second-to-die) pays only on the second death.

detail.question: In a contractual (non-contributory) group insurance plan:
options: All eligible employees are covered and the employer pays the premiums
Employees pay part of the premiums
A minimum number of employees must opt in
detail.explanation: Table: in a contractual (non-contributory) plan, all eligible employees are contractually covered and premiums are paid by the employer. A voluntary (contributory) plan requires employees to pay part.

detail.question: Which is a characteristic of GROUP insurance compared with individual insurance?
options: Premium rates are guaranteed for life
Cover is issued under a single master contract kept by the policy owner
Each member owns and controls their own policy
Every member is fully individually underwritten
detail.explanation: Para 6.2(a): group insurance is issued under a single master contract kept by the policy owner (e.g. employer). When a member leaves or dies, only that member's cover ends; the plan continues.

detail.question: Which is NOT one of the ways of classifying life insurance products in the M9 syllabus?
detail.explanation: Ch 3 classifications are by statutory insurance fund, product type, premium type, and ownership. Distribution channel is not one of them. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: Term insurance is best described as:
options: A policy that always builds cash value
A savings policy with a maturity value
Pure protection that pays only on death (or TPD if included) within the term, with no cash value
detail.explanation: Ch 4 s2: term insurance is pure protection — it pays only if the insured event occurs within the term. It has no maturity benefit and no cash value.

detail.question: A decreasing term policy used to cover a housing loan has a sum assured that:
options: Falls broadly in line with the outstanding loan balance
detail.explanation: Ch 4 s2 / Ch 2 s1.22: mortgage reducing term cover falls broadly in step with the reducing loan balance.

detail.question: A renewable term policy lets the owner:
options: Convert to whole life at any time
Extend cover for a further term at expiry without fresh evidence of health
Reduce premiums by proving good health
detail.explanation: Ch 4 s2(D): renewability guarantees a further term at expiry without new health evidence, though at the premium for the attained (older) age. Convertibility is a separate option.

detail.question: The non-forfeiture option that gives a REDUCED amount of paid-up whole life cover, lasting until death with no further premiums, is:
detail.explanation: Para 3.14: reduced paid-up insurance gives a reduced amount of whole life cover, payable on the same conditions, lasting until death with no further premiums. 'Lifelong / until death' = reduced paid-up.

detail.question: The non-forfeiture option that keeps the FULL original sum assured but only for a fixed period is:
detail.explanation: Para 3.15: extended term insurance provides paid-up term cover equal to the original face value (plus bonuses, minus indebtedness) for a fixed length of term. 'Extended TERM' = fixed period, never lifelong.

detail.question: If a policy owner defaults on premiums and does not elect a non-forfeiture option within the specified period, which option usually takes effect automatically?
options: The policy simply lapses with no value
detail.explanation: Para 3.16: if the insured fails to elect an option within a specified period after default, the extended term insurance option automatically goes into effect.

detail.question: Extended term insurance is MOST appropriate when:
options: The need for protection will end in the future and the owner prefers to stop paying premiums
The owner wants to keep paying premiums
The owner needs to increase cover
detail.explanation: Para 3.15: extended term is most appropriate where the need for protection will end in future but the owner prefers not to pay any more premiums.

detail.question: An endowment policy pays the sum assured:
options: Only on death during the term
On death during the term OR survival to maturity
detail.explanation: Ch 4 s4: endowment combines protection and savings — it pays on death within the term or on survival to maturity, which is why it costs more than term.

detail.question: For pure protection at the lowest initial premium, the most suitable basic policy is:
detail.explanation: Ch 4: term insurance has no savings element, so its premium per dollar of cover is the lowest — ideal for temporary protection on a tight budget.

detail.question: Which documents must be given to a client at the point of sale of a life policy under MAS requirements?
detail.explanation: Ch 4 s5: at point of sale the adviser provides the Product Summary and Policy Illustration (plus Your Guide to Life Insurance). The contract is issued later.

detail.question: A rider (supplementary benefit) has a term that:
options: Is set independently of the basic policy
Must not extend beyond the basic policy's term
May extend beyond the basic policy's term
Is always equal to the basic policy's term
detail.explanation: Ch 5: a rider cannot outlast the basic policy — its term can be shorter than, but never longer than, the basic policy's term.

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium (TPD) rider usually has a waiting period of:
detail.explanation: Para 1.18: there is usually a six-month waiting period before premiums are waived; the owner keeps paying during it to confirm the disability meets the TPD definition. (CI riders use 30-90 days — don't blur them.)

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium rider available for separate purchase is NOT applicable to which policy type?
detail.explanation: Para 1.11: the separately-purchased rider is not applicable to single premium policies — there are no recurring premiums to waive. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: A person has a S$200,000 whole life policy with a 50% Acceleration Benefit CI rider. On a covered CI diagnosis, the insurer pays:
options: S$100,000, then the rider ends and the balance is paid on death/TPD
detail.explanation: Para 1.38: 50% x S$200,000 = S$100,000 paid on CI (plus 50% of bonuses); the rider terminates and the balance is paid on death or TPD. Total ever paid stays at the basic sum assured.

detail.question: A person buys a S$100,000 whole life policy with a S$200,000 Additional Benefit CI rider. He contracts a covered CI and later dies. The total paid is:
detail.explanation: Paras 1.45-1.46: S$200,000 is paid on CI, and the S$100,000 basic sum assured is paid on death. Total = S$300,000 (two separate pots).

detail.question: The survival period (usually 30 days) under a Critical Illness rider applies to:
detail.explanation: Para 1.87: the survival period is applicable only to the Additional Benefit type of Critical Illness rider. Acceleration has none.

detail.question: The critical illness definitions on the industry standard list, across all insurers in Singapore, cover how many illnesses?
detail.explanation: Paras 1.56-1.57: since 2003 the industry standardised definitions for a list of 37 severe critical illnesses. The figure 30 was the old per-plan cap, abolished in 2014.

detail.question: Which is NOT a typical exclusion under a Critical Illness rider?
options: A first diagnosis of a covered illness meeting the policy definition
detail.explanation: Para 1.51: a first-incidence covered illness meeting the definition is exactly what the rider pays for, not an exclusion. Pre-existing, self-inflicted and HIV are all exclusions. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: When the first annual instalment of a TPD rider benefit begins, all other riders terminate EXCEPT the:
detail.explanation: Para 1.32: the moment the first TPD instalment starts, all riders terminate, with the sole exception of the Extended TPD (ETPD) rider.

detail.question: An Accidental Death Benefit rider paying an extra amount equal to the basic sum assured on accidental death is known as providing:
detail.explanation: Ch 5 (ADB): because the extra equals the basic sum assured, total payout on accidental death doubles — 'double indemnity'. The accident must be by external, violent and visible means.

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider keeps a policy in force by waiving premiums when the life insured is:
options: Totally and permanently disabled, or suffering a covered critical illness
Unemployed for more than six months
Travelling overseas for an extended period
detail.explanation: Para 1.7: the rider keeps a policy in force when the life insured is unable to pay premiums because he is totally and permanently disabled or is suffering from one of the critical illnesses covered. Para 1.8 gives the two covered-event groups: TPD and Critical Illnesses.

detail.question: When a Waiver of Premium Rider is 'folded in' to a policy as an integral part:
options: The policy owner pays a separate rider premium each year
No additional premium is required, as it is costed into the gross premium
It applies only to single premium policies
It can be removed at the policy owner's request
detail.explanation: Paras 1.9-1.10: some insurers issue the rider folded in, in which case no additional premium is required as it has already been costed into the gross premium for the basic policy.

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider available for separate purchase is NOT applicable to which policy type?
detail.explanation: Para 1.11: the rider is available as one to be purchased separately by the proposer, but this is not applicable to single premium policies (there are no recurring premiums to waive).

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider expires when the life insured reaches:
options: The end of the premium payment term only
A specified age, usually 60 or 65 years
detail.explanation: Para 1.14: the rider expires when the life insured reaches a specified age (usually 60 or 65). Disability after the expiry age is not waived.

detail.question: Under the common TPD definition in the Waiver of Premium Rider, total and permanent disability means the life insured:
options: Cannot do any work, occupation or profession to earn wages, at the time disability commences or any time thereafter
Cannot perform his own previous occupation
Is hospitalised for more than 30 days
Has been unemployed for at least a year
detail.explanation: Para 1.16: TPD is disability such that there is, neither at the time disability commences nor at any time thereafter, any work, occupation or profession the life insured can ever do to earn any wages, compensation or profit. It is any work, ever, not just his own job.

detail.question: The usual waiting period before premiums are waived under the Waiver of Premium (TPD) rider is:
detail.explanation: Para 1.18: there is usually a waiting period of six months before the insurer waives premiums. The policy owner keeps paying during it, to make sure the disability meets the TPD definition.

detail.question: Regarding premiums paid during the Waiver of Premium waiting period, the textbook states that:
options: All insurers always refund them in full
They are never affected because no premium is due during the wait
Practice varies by insurer, and usually there is no refund of premiums already paid
They are converted into bonus units
detail.explanation: Para 1.19: some insurers refund monthly premiums paid during the waiting period once disability is confirmed, others waive only from the following policy anniversary; practices differ, and there is usually no refund of premiums already paid. Confirm the specific insurer's practice.

detail.question: Which is a commonly excluded cause under the Waiver of Premium Rider?
options: Disability from a covered illness contracted at work
Intentionally self-inflicted injuries, whether sane or insane
detail.explanation: Para 1.21: excluded causes include intentionally self-inflicted injuries (sane or insane); aircraft travel except as a fare-paying passenger on a scheduled commercial flight; injury in military/naval/air-force service during war; and injury while committing a crime.

detail.question: A Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider should NOT be recommended when the coverage already provides:
options: 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness
A 50% acceleration benefit on critical illness
An additional benefit critical illness rider
detail.explanation: Para 1.24: with 100% acceleration of the death benefit on CI, the sum assured is paid out and the policy terminates on a CI claim, so there would be no premiums left to waive.

detail.question: When the first annual instalment of a TPD Rider benefit begins, what happens to the other riders on the policy?
options: All are terminated, except the Extended TPD (ETPD) Rider
Only the critical illness rider continues
All including the ETPD rider are terminated
detail.explanation: Para 1.32: the moment the first annual instalment payment starts, all riders under the policy are terminated, with the exception of the Extended Total and Permanent Disability (ETPD) Rider.

detail.question: Most insurers place an aggregate limit on the total TPD benefit payable on all of an insured's policies with them, usually of:
detail.explanation: Para 1.34: most insurers place a limit (usually S$2,000,000) on the aggregate TPD benefit across all policies an insured holds with the insurer, and will not pay if disability is from intentional self-inflicted injury.

detail.question: An Acceleration Benefit Critical Illness Rider provides for:
options: A payment on top of the basic sum assured
A refund of premiums on diagnosis
The pre-payment of a portion or the full sum assured of the basic policy
detail.explanation: Para 1.38: the acceleration (accelerator/enhancer) rider provides for the pre-payment of a portion (e.g. 50%) or the full sum assured (100%) of the basic policy to which it is attached.

detail.question: A person has a S$200,000 whole life policy with a 50% Acceleration Benefit CI Rider. On diagnosis of a covered critical illness, the insurer pays:
options: S$100,000 (plus 50% of any bonuses); the rider then terminates and the balance is paid on death or TPD
detail.explanation: Para 1.38: the insurer pays S$100,000 (50% x S$200,000) plus 50% of bonuses if any; the CI rider then terminates, and the balance of the sum assured plus bonuses is paid on death or TPD.

detail.question: If a person chooses a 100% Acceleration Benefit and makes a valid critical illness claim:
options: The basic sum assured remains intact for death
The full sum assured is paid and the whole policy terminates
Half the sum assured is paid and half remains
The rider converts to an additional benefit type
detail.explanation: Para 1.40: with a 100% Acceleration Benefit, the full sum assured (plus bonuses if any) is paid and the whole policy is terminated. A CI claim can be made only once (para 1.39).

detail.question: Under an Additional Benefit Critical Illness Rider, on diagnosis of a covered critical illness:
options: Part of the basic sum assured is advanced
Only the rider sum assured is paid; the basic sum assured remains intact for a later insured event
Both the basic and rider sums are paid at once
detail.explanation: Paras 1.43-1.44: the rider pays in addition to the basic plan; on diagnosis only the rider sum assured is paid, and the basic sum assured remains intact, paid only when an insured event such as death occurs.

detail.question: A person buys a S$100,000 whole life policy with a S$200,000 Additional Benefit CI Rider. He contracts a covered critical illness and later dies. The total the insurer pays is:
detail.explanation: Paras 1.45-1.46: S$200,000 is paid on the critical illness and the S$100,000 basic sum assured is paid on death, so the total is S$300,000.

detail.question: Regarding the sum assured limit, an Acceleration Benefit CI rider's sum assured:
options: Must not exceed that of the basic policy
Can be up to five times the basic sum assured
Must equal the basic sum assured exactly
detail.explanation: Table 5.1 (para 1.48): the acceleration CI rider sum assured must not exceed that of the basic policy, whereas the additional benefit rider can be up to a certain multiple of the basic sum assured.

detail.question: The critical illness definitions for the standard list are, across all insurers in Singapore:
options: Fixed and standardised, covering a list of 37 critical illnesses
detail.explanation: Paras 1.56-1.57: definitions of the listed diseases are fixed across all insurers in Singapore; since 2003 the industry standardised definitions for a list of 37 severe critical illnesses (reviewed 2014 and 2019).

detail.question: The survival period requirement (usually 30 days from the covered event) applies to which type of Critical Illness Rider?
detail.explanation: Para 1.87: the life insured must survive the survival period (usually 30 days from the date of occurrence). This survival period is applicable only to the Additional Benefit type of Critical Illness Rider.

detail.question: Which situation involves a PURE risk?
options: Placing a bet on a football match
A driver colliding with another car
detail.explanation: A car collision has only loss or no loss, no chance of gain: pure risk, insurable. Betting, product launches and currency trading all carry a possibility of gain, so they are speculative.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, pure vs speculative risk

detail.question: Which is NOT one of the ways insurers combat anti-selection?
options: Guaranteeing acceptance with no health questions
detail.explanation: Guaranteed acceptance INVITES anti-selection, it doesn't combat it. Underwriting, excesses and pre-existing exclusions are the three defences.

detail.question: A key difference between insurance and gambling is that:
options: Insurance creates a new risk; gambling transfers an existing one
Insurance transfers an existing pure risk; gambling creates a new speculative one
detail.explanation: Insurance handles a pre-existing pure risk and restores the insured; gambling manufactures a new speculative risk and is socially unproductive.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurance vs gambling

detail.question: Under utmost good faith, which fact must a proposer disclose?
options: A fact that reduces the risk
A previous serious illness that increases the risk
A fact the insurer already knows
detail.explanation: Material facts that increase the risk must be disclosed. Facts of law, risk-reducing facts, and facts the insurer already knows are exempt from disclosure.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, utmost good faith

detail.question: Being significantly overweight, which raises the chance of a health claim, is an example of:
detail.explanation: A bodily condition raising the chance of loss is a physical hazard. Moral hazard is about dishonesty; anti-selection is about who chooses to buy.

detail.question: A consumer is unhappy with how a claim was handled and mediation has failed. The next step within FIDReC is:
options: Referral to MAS for a ruling
detail.explanation: FIDReC's process is mediation first, then adjudication if unresolved. MAS regulates but does not adjudicate individual disputes; SDIC handles insurer insolvency.

detail.question: The principle of indemnity means an insured:
options: Can profit from a covered loss
Is restored to their financial position before the loss, no more
Must always accept a cash settlement
Loses the right to sue a negligent third party
detail.explanation: Indemnity restores the insured to their pre-loss position without profit. (Life policies are benefit policies, an exception to strict indemnity.)

detail.question: An insurer expects claims of S$800 per policy, S$70 of investment income, and needs S$140 of expenses and profit. The net premium is:
detail.explanation: Net = claims minus investment income = 800 - 70 = S$730. (Gross would then be 730 + 140 = S$870, the tempting wrong answer.)

detail.question: CPF savings may be used to buy which premium type(s)?
options: Single premium and recurrent single premium
detail.explanation: CPF monies can buy only single premium or recurrent single premium policies.

detail.question: Which statement about a SINGLE premium policy is correct?
options: It is paid in yearly instalments and can lapse
The entire premium is paid once, and it is cheaper than the sum of equivalent regular premiums
It is only available for term insurance
detail.explanation: Single premium: one payment, cheaper overall because the insurer invests it all upfront. Recurring-but-never-lapsing describes recurrent single premium, a different mode.

detail.question: All of the following usually reduce the premium rate EXCEPT:
detail.explanation: Monthly payment costs about 2% MORE than annual, so it raises, not reduces, the effective premium. Non-smoker status, larger sum assured and being female all reduce the rate.

detail.question: For HEALTH insurance, which statement about gender is correct?
options: Women always pay less than men
Women above 65 tend to pay more, reflecting higher hospitalisation
Gender is never used in health pricing
detail.explanation: The health flip: women above 65 tend to pay more due to higher hospitalisation rates, the reverse of the life-insurance pattern.

detail.question: Why do larger policies often get a discount on the premium rate per S$1,000 of cover?
options: Fixed administration costs are spread over more cover
detail.explanation: Admin costs are largely fixed regardless of size, so spreading them over a bigger sum lowers the per-S$1,000 cost. Nothing to do with mortality or health.

detail.question: Which policy type is NOT a statutory insurance fund category under the Insurance Act?
detail.explanation: The three statutory fund categories are participating, non-participating and investment-linked. 'Group' is an ownership arrangement, not a fund category.

detail.question: A Universal Life policy is:
options: A participating whole life paying guaranteed bonuses
A non-par, interest-sensitive plan with a declared crediting rate and flexible premiums
A term policy with a savings rider
Always sold on a group basis
detail.explanation: Universal Life is non-par, interest-sensitive, with a declared crediting rate (guaranteed minimum) and flexible premiums in amount, method and timing.

detail.question: A wealthy couple wants a policy paying out only on the death of the second spouse, to fund estate duties. This is:
detail.explanation: Payment on the second death, for estate planning, is a last survivor / second-to-die policy. First-to-die pays on the first death.

detail.question: Which is an advantage of a voluntary (contributory) group plan over a contractual one?
options: Automatic cover for all eligible employees
Employees share the cost, reducing the employer's outlay
detail.explanation: In a voluntary plan employees pay part of the cost. Low admin, auto-cover and no minimum-participation requirement are features of the contractual plan.

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT true of a third-party policy?
options: The policy owner and life insured are different people
The roles of owner and life insured are unequal
A payor benefit rider may waive premiums if the owner dies
The owner and life insured are always spouses insured jointly
detail.explanation: Two spouses insured jointly describes joint life, not third-party. Third-party = different owner and life insured with unequal roles, often with a payor benefit rider.

detail.question: Group insurance premiums are generally cheaper than individual cover mainly because:
options: Group underwriting and one master contract lower administration and selection costs
detail.explanation: One master contract plus group underwriting/experience rating cuts admin and selection costs, making group cover cheaper. Not a subsidy or an age effect.

detail.question: Whole Life and Endowment policies share which characteristic that Term does not?
options: They can be issued as participating policies
They must be kept in a separate fund
detail.explanation: Whole Life and Endowment can be participating; Term is non-par only. Separate funds apply to ILPs, and endowments also pay on survival.

detail.question: A homeowner wants cover that falls in step with a 20-year housing loan. The suitable term shape is:
detail.explanation: A shrinking loan needs shrinking cover: decreasing term (mortgage reducing term). Draw the need as a falling line, match the falling shape.

detail.question: Which statement about a mortgage decreasing term policy is correct?
options: The sum assured always equals the exact outstanding loan
The sum assured follows a schedule fixed at outset using an assumed interest rate
The premium rises each year with the loan
It pays a maturity value if the borrower survives
detail.explanation: The reducing schedule is fixed at outset on assumptions, so the payout tracks but need not exactly equal the actual loan. It is pure protection with no maturity value.

detail.question: Which non-forfeiture choice provides the FULL original sum assured, but only for a limited period, with no further premiums?
detail.explanation: Full sum, fixed period = extended term (tall-and-short). 'Extended TERM' contains term = fixed period. Reduced paid-up keeps lifetime cover but a smaller sum.

detail.question: A policyholder stops paying but makes no election. Under the automatic premium loan provision, the insurer will:
options: Immediately surrender the policy for cash
Advance the cash value as a loan (with interest) to pay the premiums due
Convert the policy to extended term at once
detail.explanation: APL lends against the cash value, with interest, to keep premiums paid until the value is exhausted. Extended term is the later fallback if nothing is elected and the value runs out.

detail.question: 'Whole Life' in the product name refers to:
options: The period over which premiums are paid
The duration of the insurance protection
The length of the free-look period
The time until the policy matures at 65
detail.explanation: 'Whole Life' describes lifelong PROTECTION, not the premium-paying period. Ordinary vs limited-payment whole life differ only in how long you pay.

detail.question: A Limited Premium Payment Whole Life policy 'paid-up at 65' means:
options: Premiums stop at 65 but cover continues for life
The maturity value is paid at 65
The policy converts to term at 65
detail.explanation: Paid-up at 65 = premiums cease at 65; lifelong cover continues. Don't confuse premium term with coverage term.

detail.question: Which need is best matched to an endowment policy?
options: Providing a lump sum for a child's university fees in 15 years
Cheapest possible protection for a fixed term
detail.explanation: A dated savings goal with protection meanwhile = endowment. Decreasing loan = decreasing term; cheapest fixed-term = term; lifelong = whole life.

detail.question: Among term, whole life and endowment, which typically has the HIGHEST premium for the same sum assured, and why?
options: Whole life, because it lasts a lifetime
Endowment, because a payout is almost certain (death or maturity)
detail.explanation: Endowment is dearest because someone is paid in every outcome, so the insurer will almost certainly pay. Term is cheapest (may pay nothing); whole life sits between.

detail.question: Which document originates from the LIA rather than a MAS notice?
detail.explanation: 'Your Guide To Life Insurance' comes from the LIA. The Product Summary, Policy Illustration and Product Highlights Sheet are tied to MAS notices (FAA-N16 / Notice 307).

detail.question: On an online no-advice platform, before purchase the client must acknowledge a self-assessment (Annex A) covering:
detail.explanation: The Annex A self-check covers affordability (via the MoneySense budget calculator), whether a Direct Purchase Insurance product would suit, and general suitability.

detail.question: A factory owner insures his warehouse against fire. Six months later he sells the warehouse but keeps the policy running. It burns down the next week. Why is his claim likely to fail?
options: He no longer has insurable interest at the time of loss
The policy lapsed automatically on sale
detail.explanation: General insurance requires insurable interest at BOTH inception and the time of loss. He had it at inception but lost it when he sold, so at the moment of the fire he had none.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurable interest timing (general)

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement of an insurable risk?
options: The loss must be measurable in money
There must be a large number of similar exposure units
The loss must offer the chance of financial gain
detail.explanation: A chance of gain makes a risk speculative and UNinsurable. Insurable risks are pure risks: measurable, numerous, fortuitous, not catastrophic to the insurer. Read 'NOT' carefully.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, requirements of insurable risk

detail.question: An agent tells a client a minor detail is unimportant and not to bother disclosing it. The client relies on this. The doctrine that may stop the insurer denying the claim later is:
detail.explanation: Waiver: the insurer, through its agent, gave up the right to that information, so it cannot later rely on non-disclosure of it. Indemnity/subrogation/contribution are unrelated claim-settlement principles.

detail.question: SDIC's policy owners' protection scheme is designed to:
options: Protect policy owners if an insurer fails, within scheme limits
detail.explanation: The Policy Owners' Protection Scheme (run by SDIC) protects policyholders if an insurer becomes insolvent, subject to caps. It doesn't guarantee returns or cover mis-selling.

detail.question: The likelihood that a policyholder will act dishonestly, such as staging a loss, is called:
detail.explanation: Moral hazard concerns dishonest conduct or intent. Physical hazard is a bodily/property characteristic; anti-selection is worse-than-average risks being keener to buy.

detail.question: Which pair correctly matches the market body to its role?
options: LIA regulates insurers; MAS is an industry association
MAS is the regulator; LIA is the life insurers' industry association
FIDReC sets premium rates; SDIC mediates disputes
GIA regulates life insurance; LIA regulates general insurance
detail.explanation: MAS is the statutory regulator; LIA is the Life Insurance Association (industry body). FIDReC resolves disputes, SDIC runs protection schemes, GIA is the general insurance association.

detail.question: A man buys a life policy on his business partner, on whom the business depends financially. At what point must insurable interest exist?
options: At the time of the partner's death only
Insurable interest is not required between business partners
detail.explanation: Life insurance requires insurable interest at inception only. A business partner with financial dependency is a recognised insurable-interest relationship.

detail.question: The loading added to a net premium covers all EXCEPT:
options: An allowance for policies that lapse early
The mortality cost of the risk
detail.explanation: Mortality cost is part of the NET premium, not the loading. Loading covers expenses, commission, lapse allowance and profit. This is your net-vs-gross distinction as a NOT-question.

detail.question: A policy owner wants to pay for his whole life plan over just 15 years instead of for life, then stop. Compared with paying for life, each premium will be:
options: Lower, because fewer years of cover
Higher, because the same cost is compressed into fewer payments
The same, because cover is identical
Zero after year 15 with no effect on cost
detail.explanation: Limited payment compresses the same lifetime cost into fewer instalments, so each premium is higher. Cover still runs for life.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 2, payment modes / Ch 4 limited payment

detail.question: Which premium mode consists of a series of stand-alone single premiums, where stopping at any time leaves the policy fully paid-up rather than lapsed?
detail.explanation: Recurrent single premium: each payment is its own single premium buying a paid-up chunk, so stopping never causes a lapse. A single premium is ONE payment only; regular premium lapses if unpaid.

detail.question: Which factor would NOT normally affect the premium an insurer charges for a life policy?
options: The applicant's occupation as a librarian versus an accountant, both low-risk
detail.explanation: Between two equally low-risk desk occupations, occupation makes no rating difference. Smoking, sum assured and payment frequency are all listed pricing factors.

detail.question: An insurer assumes a 4% investment return when pricing. Actual returns come in at 2%. Who bears the shortfall on a guaranteed non-par policy?
options: The policyholder, via a higher premium next year
detail.explanation: Premiums are priced on the assumed rate; the insurer carries the gap if actual returns fall short on guaranteed non-par business. Non-par has no bonus to cut.

detail.question: For a whole life policy, a woman aged 30 is typically charged a premium based on the mortality of:
detail.explanation: Women live longer, so 'reduction in age' prices a woman as a younger man for life cover. (Health insurance flips this above 65.)

detail.question: Which statement about statutory insurance funds is correct?
options: All three fund types must be kept separate from each other
Participating and non-participating may share a fund; investment-linked must be separate
Investment-linked may share with participating; non-par must be separate
Funds may be freely combined at the insurer's discretion
detail.explanation: Par and non-par MAY share a fund; ILP MUST be separate. The must-versus-may line is the trap.

detail.question: Which of the following can NOT be issued as a participating policy?
detail.explanation: Term (and Universal Life) are non-par only, with no bonus-sharing surplus. Whole Life, Endowment and anticipated endowment can be participating.

detail.question: A married couple wants one policy that pays on the FIRST death, to clear their joint housing loan. This is a:
detail.explanation: Two lives, pays on the first death, cover then ends: first-to-die joint life, the classic joint-mortgage structure. Last survivor pays on the second death.

detail.question: Under a non-contributory (contractual) group scheme:
options: Employees pay part of the premium and may opt out
The employer pays all premiums and all eligible employees are automatically covered
The insurer requires a minimum participation rate
detail.explanation: Non-contributory = employer pays everything, everyone eligible is auto-covered. Cost-sharing, opting out and minimum-participation belong to the voluntary/contributory plan.

detail.question: Which is NOT a typical feature of group insurance compared with individual insurance?
options: One master contract covers many lives
Cover generally ceases when a member leaves the group
Each member selects their own sum assured and is individually underwritten
It is usually cheaper than equivalent individual cover
detail.explanation: Group insurance uses group underwriting and employer-set cover, not individual selection and underwriting. The other three are genuine group features.

detail.question: A grandmother takes out and owns an education endowment on her grandchild's life. This ownership type is:
detail.explanation: Owner (grandmother) differs from the life insured (grandchild) with unequal roles: third-party. Single life would need owner and life insured to be the same person.

detail.question: Since 1 October 2001, when selling group insurance products, a representative must:
options: Obtain MAS pre-approval for each policy
Offer a free-look period of 60 days
detail.explanation: Since 1 Oct 2001 reps must complete a fact-find when selling group products. The other options are unrelated requirements.

detail.question: A company insures a key executive whose value to the firm is expected to stay level for 10 years. Best policy and beneficiary:
detail.explanation: Level need = level term; the company suffers the financial loss so it is the beneficiary. Key-person insurance is level term with the company as beneficiary.

detail.question: Which statement about the renewable option on a term policy is FALSE?
options: Renewal requires no evidence of insurability
The premium rises to reflect attained age
The sum assured is reduced at each renewal
Renewals are usually allowed only up to about age 60
detail.explanation: The sum assured is NOT reduced; renewal keeps the same benefit and term, only the premium rises. The other three are true. This is a NOT/FALSE question, mark each one.

detail.question: Mdm Lee, 60, stops paying her whole life premiums. She wants cover to continue for the rest of her life with no further payments, accepting a smaller sum. The right non-forfeiture option is:
detail.explanation: Lifelong cover, no further premiums, reduced amount = reduced paid-up (long-and-low). 'Lifelong' rules out extended term, which is a fixed period.

detail.question: A policyholder wants to keep his FULL sum assured after stopping premiums, and doesn't mind that cover will run only for a fixed number of years. He should choose:
detail.explanation: Full sum for a fixed period = extended term (tall-and-short). Reduced paid-up keeps lifetime cover but shrinks the sum. This is the mirror image of the reduced paid-up question.

detail.question: Which is NOT a condition of converting a term policy to a permanent one?
options: A written request with the premium
New sum assured not exceeding the original
Original policy exclusions continue to apply
detail.explanation: Evidence of insurability is NOT required, that waiver is the point of the option. The other three are genuine conditions.

detail.question: A whole life policy accumulates a cash value primarily because:
options: The insurer adds an annual savings bonus
Level premiums exceed the true cost of risk in the early years, and the excess is invested
Policyholders pay a separate investment premium
MAS mandates a minimum cash value
detail.explanation: Level premium on a rising risk means early overpayment, which is banked and invested: that is the cash value. It's structural, not an added feature.

detail.question: An endowment policy is best suited to a client who wants:
options: A lump sum at a specific future date, plus protection until then
Lifelong cover with no savings element
Cover that decreases with a loan
detail.explanation: Endowment matches a dated goal (education, retirement target) with protection meanwhile: maturity value on survival, sum assured on earlier death. Cheapest death-only = term; lifelong = whole life; loan-linked = decreasing term.

detail.question: Mr Tan's anticipated endowment (sum assured S$40,000) has paid him S$12,000 in cashbacks. He dies before maturity. His beneficiaries receive:
detail.explanation: Full S$40,000 sum assured. Cashbacks already received are not deducted from the death benefit.

detail.question: When recommending a plain participating whole life policy (not bundled, not an ILP), which document is NOT required?
detail.explanation: The Product Highlights Sheet is required only for ILPs. A plain par whole life needs the Cover Page, Product Summary and Policy Illustration (plus the LIA Guide).

detail.question: On an online no-advice platform, a term policy is issued with a pre-existing-condition exclusion added at underwriting. Before the client can buy, the platform must:
options: Highlight the conditional acceptance and obtain the client's acknowledgement
Refer the client to a human adviser
detail.explanation: Conditional acceptance (exclusions or loadings from underwriting) must be highlighted and acknowledged before purchase on a no-advice platform.

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider keeps a policy in force by waiving premiums when the life insured is:
options: Totally and permanently disabled, or suffering a covered critical illness
Unemployed for more than six months
Travelling overseas for an extended period
detail.explanation: Para 1.7: the rider keeps a policy in force when the life insured is unable to pay premiums because he is totally and permanently disabled or is suffering from one of the critical illnesses covered. Para 1.8 gives the two covered-event groups: TPD and Critical Illnesses.

detail.question: When a Waiver of Premium Rider is 'folded in' to a policy as an integral part:
options: The policy owner pays a separate rider premium each year
No additional premium is required, as it is costed into the gross premium
It applies only to single premium policies
It can be removed at the policy owner's request
detail.explanation: Paras 1.9-1.10: some insurers issue the rider folded in, in which case no additional premium is required as it has already been costed into the gross premium for the basic policy.

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider available for separate purchase is NOT applicable to which policy type?
detail.explanation: Para 1.11: the rider is available as one to be purchased separately by the proposer, but this is not applicable to single premium policies (there are no recurring premiums to waive).

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider expires when the life insured reaches:
options: The end of the premium payment term only
A specified age, usually 60 or 65 years
detail.explanation: Para 1.14: the rider expires when the life insured reaches a specified age (usually 60 or 65). Disability after the expiry age is not waived.

detail.question: Under the common TPD definition in the Waiver of Premium Rider, total and permanent disability means the life insured:
options: Cannot do any work, occupation or profession to earn wages, at the time disability commences or any time thereafter
Cannot perform his own previous occupation
Is hospitalised for more than 30 days
Has been unemployed for at least a year
detail.explanation: Para 1.16: TPD is disability such that there is, neither at the time disability commences nor at any time thereafter, any work, occupation or profession the life insured can ever do to earn any wages, compensation or profit. It is any work, ever, not just his own job.

detail.question: The usual waiting period before premiums are waived under the Waiver of Premium (TPD) rider is:
detail.explanation: Para 1.18: there is usually a waiting period of six months before the insurer waives premiums. The policy owner keeps paying during it, to make sure the disability meets the TPD definition.

detail.question: Regarding premiums paid during the Waiver of Premium waiting period, the textbook states that:
options: All insurers always refund them in full
They are never affected because no premium is due during the wait
Practice varies by insurer, and usually there is no refund of premiums already paid
They are converted into bonus units
detail.explanation: Para 1.19: some insurers refund monthly premiums paid during the waiting period once disability is confirmed, others waive only from the following policy anniversary; practices differ, and there is usually no refund of premiums already paid. Confirm the specific insurer's practice.

detail.question: Which is a commonly excluded cause under the Waiver of Premium Rider?
options: Disability from a covered illness contracted at work
Intentionally self-inflicted injuries, whether sane or insane
detail.explanation: Para 1.21: excluded causes include intentionally self-inflicted injuries (sane or insane); aircraft travel except as a fare-paying passenger on a scheduled commercial flight; injury in military/naval/air-force service during war; and injury while committing a crime.

detail.question: A Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider should NOT be recommended when the coverage already provides:
options: 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness
A 50% acceleration benefit on critical illness
An additional benefit critical illness rider
detail.explanation: Para 1.24: with 100% acceleration of the death benefit on CI, the sum assured is paid out and the policy terminates on a CI claim, so there would be no premiums left to waive.

detail.question: When the first annual instalment of a TPD Rider benefit begins, what happens to the other riders on the policy?
options: All are terminated, except the Extended TPD (ETPD) Rider
Only the critical illness rider continues
All including the ETPD rider are terminated
detail.explanation: Para 1.32: the moment the first annual instalment payment starts, all riders under the policy are terminated, with the exception of the Extended Total and Permanent Disability (ETPD) Rider.

detail.question: Most insurers place an aggregate limit on the total TPD benefit payable on all of an insured's policies with them, usually of:
detail.explanation: Para 1.34: most insurers place a limit (usually S$2,000,000) on the aggregate TPD benefit across all policies an insured holds with the insurer, and will not pay if disability is from intentional self-inflicted injury.

detail.question: An Acceleration Benefit Critical Illness Rider provides for:
options: A payment on top of the basic sum assured
A refund of premiums on diagnosis
The pre-payment of a portion or the full sum assured of the basic policy
detail.explanation: Para 1.38: the acceleration (accelerator/enhancer) rider provides for the pre-payment of a portion (e.g. 50%) or the full sum assured (100%) of the basic policy to which it is attached.

detail.question: A person has a S$200,000 whole life policy with a 50% Acceleration Benefit CI Rider. On diagnosis of a covered critical illness, the insurer pays:
options: S$100,000 (plus 50% of any bonuses); the rider then terminates and the balance is paid on death or TPD
detail.explanation: Para 1.38: the insurer pays S$100,000 (50% x S$200,000) plus 50% of bonuses if any; the CI rider then terminates, and the balance of the sum assured plus bonuses is paid on death or TPD.

detail.question: If a person chooses a 100% Acceleration Benefit and makes a valid critical illness claim:
options: The basic sum assured remains intact for death
The full sum assured is paid and the whole policy terminates
Half the sum assured is paid and half remains
The rider converts to an additional benefit type
detail.explanation: Para 1.40: with a 100% Acceleration Benefit, the full sum assured (plus bonuses if any) is paid and the whole policy is terminated. A CI claim can be made only once (para 1.39).

detail.question: Under an Additional Benefit Critical Illness Rider, on diagnosis of a covered critical illness:
options: Part of the basic sum assured is advanced
Only the rider sum assured is paid; the basic sum assured remains intact for a later insured event
Both the basic and rider sums are paid at once
detail.explanation: Paras 1.43-1.44: the rider pays in addition to the basic plan; on diagnosis only the rider sum assured is paid, and the basic sum assured remains intact, paid only when an insured event such as death occurs.

detail.question: A person buys a S$100,000 whole life policy with a S$200,000 Additional Benefit CI Rider. He contracts a covered critical illness and later dies. The total the insurer pays is:
detail.explanation: Paras 1.45-1.46: S$200,000 is paid on the critical illness and the S$100,000 basic sum assured is paid on death, so the total is S$300,000.

detail.question: Regarding the sum assured limit, an Acceleration Benefit CI rider's sum assured:
options: Must not exceed that of the basic policy
Can be up to five times the basic sum assured
Must equal the basic sum assured exactly
detail.explanation: Table 5.1 (para 1.48): the acceleration CI rider sum assured must not exceed that of the basic policy, whereas the additional benefit rider can be up to a certain multiple of the basic sum assured.

detail.question: The critical illness definitions for the standard list are, across all insurers in Singapore:
options: Fixed and standardised, covering a list of 37 critical illnesses
detail.explanation: Paras 1.56-1.57: definitions of the listed diseases are fixed across all insurers in Singapore; since 2003 the industry standardised definitions for a list of 37 severe critical illnesses (reviewed 2014 and 2019).

detail.question: The survival period requirement (usually 30 days from the covered event) applies to which type of Critical Illness Rider?
detail.explanation: Para 1.87: the life insured must survive the survival period (usually 30 days from the date of occurrence). This survival period is applicable only to the Additional Benefit type of Critical Illness Rider.

detail.question: The Waiver of Premium Rider keeps a policy in force by waiving premiums when the life insured is:

Unemployed for more than six months
Travelling overseas for an extended period
