detail.question: A packaged product bundles a whole life policy with a critical illness rider the buyer cannot omit. Two years in, the client asks to drop only the CI rider while keeping the base cover. The insurer:
options: May decline, because in a packaged product the rider is folded in and not separately removable
Must remove the rider on request since riders are always optional
Must refund the rider premiums paid to date
Must convert the rider into an additional benefit type
detail.explanation: Riders can usually be added or removed, but where a product is sold as a package with the rider folded in, the buyer cannot pick it out. That is the stated exception to the 'riders can be removed' rule.

detail.question: A whole life policy lapses. On reinstatement, the insurer restores the base cover but declines the attached CI rider. This is:
options: Not permitted; the rider must follow the base policy automatically
Permitted; on reinstatement the insurer may refuse to bring the rider back
Permitted only if the rider had no cash value
Permitted only if premiums were unpaid for over six months
detail.explanation: Reinstating the base policy does not drag the rider back. The insurer may reinstate the base yet refuse the rider, regardless of cash value or lapse length.

detail.question: A client becomes totally and permanently disabled but is still able to earn a small income tutoring from home. Under the common TPD definition in the Waiver rider, does he meet it?
options: Yes, because he cannot do his previous job
No, because he can still do some work to earn wages
No, because tutoring is not a recognised occupation
detail.explanation: The common TPD definition requires that there be NO work, occupation or profession he can EVER do to earn any wages. Being able to earn from tutoring means he does not meet it. 'Any work, ever', not just his own job.

detail.question: A client's Waiver of Premium rider has a six-month waiting period. He is disabled and dies in month four, still paying premiums. The main purpose the waiting period was serving here was to:
options: Delay the death claim by four months
Increase the cash value before any waiver
Allow the insurer to re-underwrite the policy
Confirm the disability was permanent before waiving premiums
detail.explanation: The waiting period exists to confirm the disability genuinely meets the permanent definition before premiums are waived. Permanence takes time to prove, which is why it is longer than the CI diagnosis wait.

detail.question: Why is the Waiver/TPD waiting period (six months) longer than the Critical Illness rider waiting period (30-90 days)?
options: Permanence of disability takes longer to confirm than a diagnosis
TPD claims are larger in value
Regulations require a fixed six-month gap for all riders
CI claims are paid in instalments
detail.explanation: A diagnosis is relatively quick to confirm; proving a disability is PERMANENT needs a longer observation window. The length of each waiting period tracks how long its trigger takes to prove.

detail.question: A client holds S$1.4m TPD cover on one policy and S$900,000 on a second policy, both with the same insurer. He suffers TPD. Assuming a typical S$2m aggregate cap, the insurer pays approximately:
detail.explanation: The aggregate cap applies across all policies with that insurer. S$1.4m + S$0.9m = S$2.3m, but the payout is capped at S$2m. Splitting across policies does not beat the cap.

detail.question: A client's policy provides a 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness. His adviser suggests also adding a Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider. The best assessment is:
options: A sensible addition that protects future premiums
Required by MAS for all CI policies
Pointless, because a 100% acceleration claim terminates the policy, leaving no premiums to waive
Only useful if the policy is an endowment
detail.explanation: With 100% acceleration, a CI claim pays the full sum assured and ends the policy. There are then no future premiums to waive, so the waiver rider can never fire.

detail.question: A S$400,000 whole life policy carries a 25% Acceleration Benefit CI rider. The client is diagnosed with a covered CI, claims, then dies three years later. Total paid across both events:
detail.explanation: 25% of S$400,000 = S$100,000 on CI (rider ends), leaving S$300,000 for death. Total = S$400,000, the one basic pot, split. Acceleration never adds to the total.

detail.question: A client wants a rider whose CI payout does NOT reduce his death benefit and can be sized at three times his basic sum assured. He needs:
detail.explanation: Only the Additional Benefit type pays a separate sum that leaves the basic intact AND can be a multiple of the basic SA. Acceleration reduces the death benefit and cannot exceed the basic SA.

detail.question: A client insists on a S$500,000 CI rider attached to his S$150,000 whole life policy. Which rider type can accommodate this, and why?
options: Acceleration, because it pre-pays the sum assured
Additional Benefit, because it is a separate pot and can be a multiple of the basic
Either type, since CI riders have no size limit
Neither, because no rider may exceed S$200,000
detail.explanation: Acceleration is drawn from the S$150,000 basic pot and cannot exceed it. Only Additional Benefit, being a separate pot, can be a multiple of the basic (here over 3x).

detail.question: Which statement about the survival period on CI riders is correct?
options: It applies to both Acceleration and Additional types to prevent anti-selection
It applies only to the Acceleration type, because it pre-pays the death benefit
It applies only to the Additional Benefit type, to confirm genuine survival before paying a separate benefit
It replaces the waiting period for Multiple Pay plans
detail.explanation: The survival period applies to the Additional Benefit type only. It guards the exit (proof of survival before an extra pot pays), whereas the waiting period guards the entry (anti-selection). Acceleration merely pre-pays the death benefit, so needs no survival test.

detail.question: The waiting period at the start of a CI rider exists primarily to:
options: Confirm the insured survives the illness
Allow the cash value to build
Give the insurer time to set the premium
Prevent anti-selection by those who buy after suspecting illness
detail.explanation: The waiting period guards entry: it stops someone buying the rider only once they suspect something is wrong. Diagnosis during it voids the policy with premiums refunded. Survival is a separate, exit-side concept.

detail.question: A CI rider is issued on 1 March. The insured is diagnosed with a covered illness on 20 April, within the 90-day waiting period. The insurer will most likely:
options: Void the policy and refund premiums paid, without interest
Defer the claim until the waiting period ends, then pay
detail.explanation: Diagnosis before or during the waiting period usually leads the insurer to void the policy and refund premiums without interest, since the waiting period is there to defeat exactly this.

detail.question: Why does an Acceleration Benefit CI rider typically cover to about age 100, while an Additional Benefit CI rider caps around age 65?
options: Acceleration is cheaper so it can run longer
Acceleration is tied to lifelong whole life cover; Additional is a standalone-style top-up capped near retirement
MAS sets both ages by regulation
Additional benefits are only for children
detail.explanation: Acceleration rides on the death benefit of a whole life policy, so it can run to ~100. Additional is priced like standalone CI, which insurers cap earlier (~65) as old-age claim risk climbs.

detail.question: Two insurers define 'major cancer' with identical wording. A third covers an extra illness not on the standard list, using its own definition. Which is correct?
options: All three definitions must be identical by law
MAS writes every definition an insurer may use
The standard-list illnesses follow LIA standardised definitions; illnesses beyond the list are defined by each insurer
Only the SCI may approve any CI definition
detail.explanation: The LIA standardises definitions for the listed critical illnesses, giving consistency. For conditions beyond the standard list, each insurer sets its own definition.

detail.question: How many critical illnesses are on the LIA standard list, and what was the significance of the number 30 in this context?
options: 37 illnesses; 30 is the number of insurers who adopted them
37 illnesses; 30 was the old maximum-conditions-per-plan cap, abolished in 2014
detail.explanation: The standard list holds 37 illnesses. The figure 30 was the old cap on conditions per plan, abolished in the 2014 review, letting insurers cover any number.

detail.question: A client survives a covered heart attack and claims on his CI plan. Eighteen months later he suffers an unrelated stroke and wants to claim again. This is possible only under a:
options: Severity-based CI plan for one illness
detail.explanation: Two different, unrelated illnesses needing separate claims require a Multiple Pay plan. Severity-based deals with one illness worsening through stages, not separate illnesses.

detail.question: A plan pays 20% of the sum assured when an illness is at an early stage and the balance if it later becomes severe. This describes a:
detail.explanation: Paying partial amounts as ONE illness progresses through stages is severity-based (Early Pay). Multiple Pay is about separate claims for different illnesses.

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT among the exclusions typically applied to a Critical Illness rider?
options: Wilful misuse of drugs or alcohol
A first diagnosis of end stage kidney failure meeting the policy definition
detail.explanation: A first-incidence covered illness meeting the definition is what the rider pays for, not an exclusion. Congenital disorders, drug/alcohol misuse and HIV are all listed exclusions. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: Under a Payor Benefit rider on a child's policy, premiums are waived when:
options: The child is diagnosed with a critical illness
The policy reaches its surrender value
The child turns 18 regardless of the parent's status
The paying parent dies or becomes disabled before the child reaches the specified age
detail.explanation: Payor Benefit triggers on the PAYER's death or disability, waiving premiums until the child reaches a set age (usually 21 or 25). It protects the plan against the parent being unable to pay.

detail.question: A client with a S$150,000 term insurance policy wants an extra S$150,000 of cover for ten years. The most appropriate action is to:
options: Increase the sum assured of the existing term policy
Attach a term rider to the term policy
Add a guaranteed insurability option rider
detail.explanation: A term rider cannot attach to a term policy, because you would simply raise the term policy's sum assured. Term riders exist to add temporary cover to a PERMANENT base.

detail.question: A Family Income Benefit rider pays S$3,000/month until the end of a 20-year term. Client A dies at the start of year 3; Client B dies at the start of year 18. Which family receives more in total, and why?
options: B, because surviving longer earns more
A, because more years of income remain from the date of death
B, because the monthly amount rises over time
detail.explanation: Income runs from death to the fixed end date. A has ~18 years of payments left, B only ~3, so A's total is far larger. It is a decreasing rider: earlier death, larger total.

detail.question: A client wants cover that shrinks each year to match his outstanding home loan, attached to his whole life policy. The best fit is a:
detail.explanation: A decreasing term rider's falling sum assured tracks a shrinking mortgage, and it is cheaper than a standalone decreasing term policy. 'Shrinking need' points to decreasing.

detail.question: A client with a GIO rider is diagnosed with diabetes, then reaches his next option date. What can he do?
options: Claim a lump sum for the diabetes diagnosis
Nothing; a diagnosis cancels all remaining options
Only renew, not increase, his existing cover
Buy the additional cover at that option date without a medical, despite now being diabetic
detail.explanation: GIO lets him buy additional cover at set dates WITHOUT evidence of insurability, so his new diabetes does not block the purchase. That is exactly the value of locking in the right while healthy. GIO never pays a claim itself.

detail.question: A client skips exercising his GIO option on the age-30 date. What is the effect on the age-35 option date?
options: The age-35 option remains available; skipping one date does not cancel later ones
He must pay a penalty to keep the age-35 option
The maximum cover at 35 is halved
detail.explanation: Exercising GIO is not compulsory. Skipping one option date lets that one lapse but does not affect the right to exercise on later dates.

detail.question: A client dies of a heart attack (an illness, not an accident). His S$250,000 policy carries an Accidental Death Benefit rider at the usual amount. The insurer pays:
options: Nothing, because ADB excludes natural death
detail.explanation: ADB pays extra only on ACCIDENTAL death. A heart attack is death by illness, so only the basic S$250,000 is paid. The 'double indemnity' extra requires an accident by external, violent and visible means.

detail.question: A manual worker loses the use of one hand in a workplace accident but survives. Which rider would pay a benefit for this?
detail.explanation: AD&D extends ADB to pay a percentage of the rider sum assured for dismemberment or disablement even where the insured survives. Plain ADB pays only on accidental death.

detail.question: A Hospital Cash rider pays S$200/day. A client is hospitalised for 10 days for a covered illness and his medical plan already reimburses the full bill. The Hospital Cash rider pays:
options: Nothing, because the bill was already covered
Only the deductible portion of the bill
Half of S$2,000, because of coordination of benefits
S$2,000, because it pays a fixed daily sum regardless of actual charges
detail.explanation: Hospital Cash pays a fixed sum per day of confinement regardless of the actual bill or other cover. 10 days x S$200 = S$2,000, useful precisely to offset deductibles or lost income.

detail.question: A Hospital Cash rider pays a daily benefit and carries a lifetime cap of 730 times that daily amount. The clearest way to understand 730 is:
options: Roughly two years' worth of daily benefit (365 x 2)
The number of insurers offering the rider
The maximum age for the rider
detail.explanation: 730 = 365 x 2, i.e. about two years of daily benefit. Understanding the cap as 'two years of days' makes it logical rather than arbitrary. Once total claims hit the cap, the rider terminates.

detail.question: A TPD rider pays its benefit in five annual instalments. Once the first instalment starts, what happens to the other riders on the policy?
options: All other riders terminate, except an Extended TPD (ETPD) rider
All riders including ETPD terminate immediately
detail.explanation: The moment the first TPD instalment starts, all other riders terminate, with the sole exception of an Extended TPD (ETPD) rider. Without ETPD, the policy also ends on the last payment or at the expiry age, whichever is earlier.

detail.question: A client wants extra death cover that decreases each year to match his shrinking home loan. Which rider fits?
detail.explanation: Cover falls, need falls, loan falls. A Decreasing Term Rider tracks a shrinking mortgage and is cheaper than a standalone decreasing term policy. 'Decreasing' is the keyword pointing to mortgage.

detail.question: Which CI rider type can be sized at a multiple (e.g. 5x) of the basic policy's sum assured?
detail.explanation: Additional is a separate pot on top of the basic, so it can be a multiple of the basic SA. Acceleration is pre-paid out of the basic pot, so it cannot exceed it.

detail.question: A person has a S$100,000 whole life policy. Which rider arrangement is POSSIBLE?
options: A S$250,000 Acceleration Benefit CI rider
A S$250,000 Additional Benefit CI rider
detail.explanation: Additional is a separate pot and can be a multiple of the basic (here 2.5x), so S$250,000 is fine. Acceleration is pre-paid from the S$100,000 basic pot, so it cannot exceed S$100,000. A S$250k figure on a small policy flags Additional, not Acceleration.

detail.question: The rider sum assured of an Acceleration Benefit CI rider:
options: Can be up to five times the basic sum assured
Must always equal exactly half the basic sum assured
Must not exceed the basic policy's sum assured
detail.explanation: Because acceleration is pre-paid out of the basic sum assured, it cannot exceed it. Only the Additional Benefit type can be a multiple.

detail.question: A survival period (usually 30 days after diagnosis) applies to which CI rider type?
detail.explanation: Only the Additional Benefit type carries a survival period, because it pays a separate sum on top and the insurer wants proof of genuine survival. Acceleration just pre-pays the death benefit, so no survival test.

detail.question: A client with an Acceleration Benefit CI rider is diagnosed with a covered illness. Regarding a survival period:
options: He must survive 30 days before the claim pays
There is no survival period for this rider type
He must survive 90 days before the claim pays
He must survive six months before the claim pays
detail.explanation: Acceleration has no survival period, the money is the death benefit paid early, so there is nothing to prove. Survival periods apply to Additional Benefit only.

detail.question: Which body standardises the definitions of critical illnesses for insurers in Singapore?
detail.explanation: The LIA standardises the definitions across all insurers. Not MAS, not SCI, not the individual insurer. It is the definitions that are standardised, not the number of conditions.

detail.question: A client needs a dated lump sum for his child's university fees in 15 years. Which is the WRONG tool for this need?
options: A savings plan with a fixed maturity date
A term rider timed to end at university entry
detail.explanation: A dated lump sum need points to endowment or a maturity-dated plan. A Decreasing Term Rider is for a shrinking need like a mortgage, its falling cover is the opposite of a fixed future lump sum. Match the shape: dated lump sum = endowment; decreasing = mortgage.

detail.question: Which of the following is TRUE of all riders?
options: A rider's term cannot exceed that of the basic policy
A rider's term may extend beyond the term of the basic policy
A rider can be kept even after the basic policy is cancelled
An insurer may cancel a rider at will while premiums are paid
detail.explanation: A rider's term can be shorter than, but never longer than, the basic policy. It cannot exist without a basic policy, and the insurer cannot drop it while premiums are paid.

detail.question: A policy lapsed and is now being reinstated. Regarding the rider that was attached, the insurer:
options: Must automatically reinstate the rider with the basic policy
May reinstate the basic policy but refuse to reinstate the rider
Must pay out the rider benefit on reinstatement
Cannot reinstate the basic policy unless the rider is also reinstated
detail.explanation: On reinstatement, the insurer may bring back the basic policy yet decline the rider. Reinstating the basic does not drag the rider back automatically.

detail.question: The usual waiting period under a Waiver of Premium Rider (for TPD) is:
detail.explanation: Six months, during which the owner keeps paying to confirm the disability meets the TPD definition. Do not confuse this with the 30–90 day Critical Illness waiting period.

detail.question: The TPD benefit under a rider is often paid over five years. A common payment pattern is:
options: 60% in the first year, then 10% for four years
50% in year one and 50% in year five
10% for each of the first four years, then 60% in the fifth
detail.explanation: The textbook example is 10% + 10% + 10% + 10% + 60%. Four small instalments then a large final one.

detail.question: Most insurers cap the aggregate TPD benefit across all of an insured's policies with them at about:
detail.explanation: The usual aggregate cap is S$2,000,000 per insured across all policies with that insurer. This is the same figure as the CI sum assured cap.

detail.question: A Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider is generally pointless when the policy already has:
options: A 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness
detail.explanation: 100% acceleration pays the full sum assured on CI and terminates the policy, leaving no future premiums to waive.

detail.question: A person has a S$300,000 whole life policy with a 100% Acceleration Benefit CI Rider. He is diagnosed with a covered critical illness and claims. He dies two years later. Total paid by the insurer across both events:
detail.explanation: 100% acceleration pays the full S$300,000 on CI and terminates the policy. Nothing is left for death. Total = S$300,000, one pot paid early.

detail.question: A person has a S$120,000 whole life policy with a 50% Acceleration Benefit CI Rider. He is diagnosed with a covered critical illness. How much is paid now, and how much remains for death?
detail.explanation: 50% of S$120,000 = S$60,000 pulled forward on CI, rider ends, and the remaining S$60,000 pays on death. One pot, split.

detail.question: A person has a S$100,000 whole life policy with a S$250,000 Additional Benefit CI Rider. He contracts a covered CI, then later dies. Total paid:
detail.explanation: Additional benefit is on top. S$250,000 pays on CI, the S$100,000 basic stays intact and pays on death. Total = S$350,000.

detail.question: The KEY difference between an Acceleration and an Additional Benefit critical illness rider is that:
options: Acceleration pays on top of the sum assured; Additional pre-pays part of it
Acceleration pre-pays part of the sum assured; Additional pays on top of it
Only Additional can be attached to a whole life policy
detail.explanation: Acceleration pre-pays out of the basic sum assured (one pot). Additional pays a separate amount on top (two pots). The hinge phrase in a question tells you which.

detail.question: The rider sum assured of an Acceleration Benefit CI rider:
options: Can be up to five times the basic sum assured
Must not exceed the basic policy's sum assured
Must equal the basic sum assured exactly
detail.explanation: Because acceleration is pre-paid out of the basic sum assured, it cannot exceed it. Only the Additional Benefit type can be a multiple of the basic.

detail.question: Which body standardises critical illness definitions for insurers in Singapore?
detail.explanation: The LIA sets the standard definitions so that a listed illness means the same thing at every insurer. It is the definitions, not the number of conditions, that are standardised.

detail.question: Two clients at different insurers find that 'major cancer' is defined identically in both their policies. This is because:
options: The LIA standardises the definitions across all insurers
It is a coincidence; definitions vary by insurer
detail.explanation: The LIA standardises the definitions of the listed critical illnesses, giving consistency across insurers. Same fact as 'who standardises', reworded.

detail.question: How many critical illnesses are on the industry standard list?
detail.explanation: 37. The figure 30 was the old maximum-conditions-per-plan cap, abolished in the 2014 review.

detail.question: The survival period (usually 30 days) under a Critical Illness Rider applies to:
detail.explanation: Only the Additional Benefit type carries a survival period. The Acceleration type has none.

detail.question: A client survives a heart attack and claims. Years later he develops an unrelated major cancer and wants to claim again. Which plan allows this?
detail.explanation: Two different, unrelated illnesses needing separate claims = Multiple Pay. Severity-based is about one illness worsening through stages.

detail.question: A Severity-based (Early Pay) CI plan differs from a standard CI plan because it:
options: Pays in stages as one illness progresses from early to severe
Pays only at the end stage of an illness
detail.explanation: Severity-based pays partial amounts at earlier stages of the same illness, rather than all-or-nothing at the severe stage.

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT a common exclusion under a Critical Illness Rider?
options: A first diagnosis of a covered illness that meets the policy definition
detail.explanation: A first-incidence covered illness meeting the definition is precisely what the rider pays for, not an exclusion. The other three are all exclusions. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: Which of these is NOT among the five illnesses covered by almost every CI policy?
detail.explanation: The five near-universal CIs are major cancer, heart attack of specified severity, coronary artery bypass surgery, stroke with permanent neurological deficit, and end stage kidney failure. A broken hip is not a critical illness. Watch the word NOT.

detail.question: The waiting period on a Critical Illness Rider is imposed mainly to:
options: Prevent anti-selection by those who buy after suspecting illness
detail.explanation: The waiting period (usually 90 days) stops someone buying the rider only when they already suspect something is wrong. Diagnosis during it voids the policy with premiums refunded.

detail.question: A Payor Benefit Rider on a juvenile policy waives premiums when:
options: The parent (payer) dies or becomes disabled before the child reaches a set age
The child (life insured) is diagnosed with a critical illness
The policy acquires a cash value
detail.explanation: Payor Benefit triggers on the PAYER's death or disability, waiving premiums until the child reaches a set age (usually 21 or 25). Waiver of Premium, by contrast, triggers on the life insured's condition.

detail.question: A Term Rider can be attached to all of the following EXCEPT:
detail.explanation: A term rider attaches to a permanent policy. It cannot go on a term policy, because the buyer would simply raise that term policy's sum assured instead. Watch the word EXCEPT.

detail.question: A client with a whole life policy needs extra death cover only for the 12 years until his children finish school. The best solution is:
options: Raise the whole life sum assured permanently
Buy a second whole life policy
Attach a level term rider for that period
detail.explanation: A temporary need on a permanent base calls for a term rider, cheap and time-limited. Raising the whole life sum assured would give permanent, permanently-priced cover for a temporary need.

detail.question: Under a Family Income Benefit Rider, the total income the beneficiary receives is:
options: Larger the later the insured dies during the term
The same regardless of when death occurs
Paid as a single lump sum on death
Larger the earlier the insured dies during the term
detail.explanation: Income runs from death to a fixed end date, so dying earlier leaves more years of payments and a larger total. It is a decreasing rider.

detail.question: A Decreasing Term Rider is most commonly used to:
options: Provide a lump sum for a child's education
detail.explanation: Its falling sum assured tracks a shrinking mortgage, and it is cheaper than a standalone decreasing term policy.

detail.question: The Guaranteed Insurability Option (GIO) Rider gives the policyholder the right to:
options: Receive a lump sum on critical illness
Buy additional insurance at set dates without evidence of insurability
Receive a daily benefit while hospitalised
detail.explanation: GIO is a right to purchase more cover at set dates or life events without a medical, priced at the age of exercise. It has a purchase trigger, not a payout trigger.

detail.question: A client with a GIO rider is diagnosed with cancer. What does the GIO rider pay him?
options: Nothing — GIO is a right to buy cover, not a benefit
detail.explanation: GIO never pays out on illness. It only lets you buy more cover at set dates without a medical, which is why it is valuable while still healthy.

detail.question: An Accidental Death Benefit rider paying an extra amount equal to the basic sum assured on accidental death is called:
detail.explanation: Because the extra equals the basic sum assured, total payout doubles on accidental death, hence 'double indemnity'. The accident must be by external, violent and visible means.

detail.question: Which rider covers both accidental death AND the loss of limbs, sight, speech or hearing from an accident?
detail.explanation: AD&D extends ADB to pay a percentage of the rider sum assured for dismemberment or disablement, even if the insured survives. ADB alone pays only on accidental death.

detail.question: A Hospital Cash (Income) Benefit Rider pays:
options: A fixed daily amount for each day of hospital confinement, regardless of actual charges
A lump sum on diagnosis of a critical illness
Premiums on the insured's behalf during disability
detail.explanation: It pays a fixed sum per day of confinement (e.g. S$150/day) regardless of the actual bill, useful to cover the deductible or co-payment on a medical plan.

detail.question: Which statement about riders is TRUE?
options: A rider can have a term longer than the basic policy it is attached to
A policy owner can purchase a rider on its own without a basic policy
The insurer may refuse to reinstate a rider when the lapsed basic policy is reinstated
An insurer can drop a rider at any time even while premiums are paid
detail.explanation: On reinstatement after a lapse, the insurer may reinstate the basic policy but refuse the rider. A rider's term can never exceed the basic policy's, cannot be bought alone, and cannot be dropped by the insurer while premiums are paid.

detail.question: Under a Waiver of Premium Rider, what is the usual waiting period before the insurer waives premiums for total and permanent disability?
detail.explanation: The usual waiting period is six months, during which the policy owner keeps paying premiums to confirm the disability meets the TPD definition. The 30–90 day figure belongs to the Critical Illness Rider, not the Waiver.

detail.question: A Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider is generally NOT recommended when the policy already provides:
options: A 50% acceleration benefit on critical illness
A 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness
An additional benefit critical illness rider
detail.explanation: With 100% acceleration, the full sum assured is paid on critical illness and the policy terminates, so there are no future premiums left to waive. The waiver would never fire.

detail.question: Most insurers cap the total TPD benefit payable across all of an insured's policies with that insurer at approximately:
detail.explanation: The usual aggregate cap is S$2,000,000 across all policies the insured holds with that insurer. Splitting cover across several policies does not dodge the cap.

detail.question: A person holds a S$200,000 whole life policy with a 50% Acceleration Benefit Critical Illness Rider. He is diagnosed with a covered critical illness. How much is paid on diagnosis, and what happens to the policy?
options: S$200,000 is paid and the policy terminates
S$100,000 is paid and the rider terminates; the remaining S$100,000 is paid on death or TPD
S$100,000 is paid in addition to the S$200,000 sum assured
Nothing is paid until the insured dies
detail.explanation: Acceleration pre-pays part of the basic sum assured. 50% of S$200,000 = S$100,000 now, rider ends, and the remaining S$100,000 pays on later death or TPD. Total ever paid stays at S$200,000.

detail.question: A person buys a S$100,000 whole life policy with a S$200,000 Additional Benefit Critical Illness Rider. He contracts a covered critical illness and later dies. What is the total amount the insurer pays?
detail.explanation: Additional benefit pays on top of the basic policy. S$200,000 is paid on critical illness, the S$100,000 basic sum assured stays intact and pays on death. Total = S$300,000.

detail.question: Which body standardises the definitions of critical illnesses used by insurers in Singapore?
options: Each individual insurer sets its own
detail.explanation: The LIA standardises the definitions across all insurers, giving consumers consistency and preventing definition-shopping. It is the definitions that are standardised, not the number of conditions a plan must cover.

detail.question: How many critical illnesses are on the industry standard list adopted by Singapore insurers?
detail.explanation: The standard list holds 37 critical illnesses. The figure 30 was the old maximum-conditions-per-plan cap, abolished in the 2014 review. Some plans go beyond the list with many more conditions, but the standardised list is 37.

detail.question: The survival period requirement (usually 30 days) for a Critical Illness Rider applies to:
detail.explanation: The survival period applies only to the Additional Benefit type of Critical Illness Rider. The Acceleration Benefit type has no survival period.

detail.question: What distinguishes a Multiple Pay CI plan from a Severity-based (Early Pay) CI plan?
options: Multiple Pay pays for the same illness worsening; Severity-based pays for different illnesses
Multiple Pay pays for different or recurrent illnesses; Severity-based pays as one illness progresses through stages
Severity-based pays a lump sum only at the end stage
detail.explanation: Severity-based pays in stages as one illness worsens (early to severe). Multiple Pay allows separate claims for different or recurrent illnesses. Same illness worsening = severity; different illnesses = multiple pay.

detail.question: Which is NOT a common exclusion under a Critical Illness Rider?
options: A first diagnosis of a covered major cancer meeting the policy definition
detail.explanation: A first-incidence covered illness that meets the definition is exactly what the rider pays for, not an exclusion. Pre-existing illness, HIV/AIDS/ARC, and self-inflicted injury are all listed exclusions.

detail.question: A Term Rider CANNOT be attached to which of the following?
detail.explanation: A Term Rider attaches to a permanent policy. It cannot attach to a term policy, because the buyer could simply raise the sum assured of that term policy instead.

detail.question: Under a Family Income Benefit Rider, the LATER the breadwinner dies during the rider term, the:
options: Larger the total income the beneficiary receives
Smaller the total income the beneficiary receives
detail.explanation: It is a decreasing term rider. Dying earlier means income is paid for longer, so a larger total. Dying later leaves fewer years of payments, so a smaller total.

detail.question: The Guaranteed Insurability Option Rider gives the policy owner the right to:
options: Claim a lump sum on diagnosis of a critical illness
Buy additional insurance at set dates without evidence of insurability
Waive premiums on total and permanent disability
Receive a fixed daily benefit during hospitalisation
detail.explanation: The GIO rider is not cover itself. It is a right to purchase additional insurance at specified dates or life events without providing evidence of insurability, priced at the age of exercise.

detail.question: An Accidental Death Benefit Rider that pays an amount equal to the basic sum assured on accidental death is commonly referred to as providing:
detail.explanation: Because the additional payout equals the basic sum assured, it is called double indemnity. It can be three or more times, but the equal-amount case gives the term. The accident must meet the external, violent and visible means definition.

detail.question: Which statement about riders is TRUE?
options: A rider can have a term longer than the basic policy it is attached to
A policy owner can purchase a rider on its own without a basic policy
The insurer may refuse to reinstate a rider when the lapsed basic policy is reinstated
An insurer can drop a rider at any time even while premiums are paid
detail.explanation: On reinstatement after a lapse, the insurer may reinstate the basic policy but refuse the rider. A rider's term can never exceed the basic policy's, cannot be bought alone, and cannot be dropped by the insurer while premiums are paid.

detail.question: Under a Waiver of Premium Rider, what is the usual waiting period before the insurer waives premiums for total and permanent disability?
detail.explanation: The usual waiting period is six months, during which the policy owner keeps paying premiums to confirm the disability meets the TPD definition. The 30–90 day figure belongs to the Critical Illness Rider, not the Waiver.

detail.question: A Waiver of Premium upon Critical Illness rider is generally NOT recommended when the policy already provides:
options: A 50% acceleration benefit on critical illness
A 100% acceleration of the death benefit on critical illness
An additional benefit critical illness rider
detail.explanation: With 100% acceleration, the full sum assured is paid on critical illness and the policy terminates, so there are no future premiums left to waive. The waiver would never fire.

detail.question: Most insurers cap the total TPD benefit payable across all of an insured's policies with that insurer at approximately:
detail.explanation: The usual aggregate cap is S$2,000,000 across all policies the insured holds with that insurer. Splitting cover across several policies does not dodge the cap.

detail.question: A person holds a S$200,000 whole life policy with a 50% Acceleration Benefit Critical Illness Rider. He is diagnosed with a covered critical illness. How much is paid on diagnosis, and what happens to the policy?
options: S$200,000 is paid and the policy terminates
S$100,000 is paid and the rider terminates; the remaining S$100,000 is paid on death or TPD
S$100,000 is paid in addition to the S$200,000 sum assured
Nothing is paid until the insured dies
detail.explanation: Acceleration pre-pays part of the basic sum assured. 50% of S$200,000 = S$100,000 now, rider ends, and the remaining S$100,000 pays on later death or TPD. Total ever paid stays at S$200,000.

detail.question: A person buys a S$100,000 whole life policy with a S$200,000 Additional Benefit Critical Illness Rider. He contracts a covered critical illness and later dies. What is the total amount the insurer pays?
detail.explanation: Additional benefit pays on top of the basic policy. S$200,000 is paid on critical illness, the S$100,000 basic sum assured stays intact and pays on death. Total = S$300,000.

detail.question: Which body standardises the definitions of critical illnesses used by insurers in Singapore?
options: Each individual insurer sets its own
detail.explanation: The LIA standardises the definitions across all insurers, giving consumers consistency and preventing definition-shopping. It is the definitions that are standardised, not the number of conditions a plan must cover.

detail.question: How many critical illnesses are on the industry standard list adopted by Singapore insurers?
detail.explanation: The standard list holds 37 critical illnesses. The figure 30 was the old maximum-conditions-per-plan cap, abolished in the 2014 review. Some plans go beyond the list with many more conditions, but the standardised list is 37.

detail.question: The survival period requirement (usually 30 days) for a Critical Illness Rider applies to:
detail.explanation: The survival period applies only to the Additional Benefit type of Critical Illness Rider. The Acceleration Benefit type has no survival period.

detail.question: What distinguishes a Multiple Pay CI plan from a Severity-based (Early Pay) CI plan?
options: Multiple Pay pays for the same illness worsening; Severity-based pays for different illnesses
Multiple Pay pays for different or recurrent illnesses; Severity-based pays as one illness progresses through stages
Severity-based pays a lump sum only at the end stage
detail.explanation: Severity-based pays in stages as one illness worsens (early to severe). Multiple Pay allows separate claims for different or recurrent illnesses. Same illness worsening = severity; different illnesses = multiple pay.

detail.question: Which is NOT a common exclusion under a Critical Illness Rider?
options: A first diagnosis of a covered major cancer meeting the policy definition
detail.explanation: A first-incidence covered illness that meets the definition is exactly what the rider pays for, not an exclusion. Pre-existing illness, HIV/AIDS/ARC, and self-inflicted injury are all listed exclusions.

detail.question: A Term Rider CANNOT be attached to which of the following?
detail.explanation: A Term Rider attaches to a permanent policy. It cannot attach to a term policy, because the buyer could simply raise the sum assured of that term policy instead.

detail.question: Under a Family Income Benefit Rider, the LATER the breadwinner dies during the rider term, the:
options: Larger the total income the beneficiary receives
Smaller the total income the beneficiary receives
detail.explanation: It is a decreasing term rider. Dying earlier means income is paid for longer, so a larger total. Dying later leaves fewer years of payments, so a smaller total.

detail.question: The Guaranteed Insurability Option Rider gives the policy owner the right to:
options: Claim a lump sum on diagnosis of a critical illness
Buy additional insurance at set dates without evidence of insurability
Waive premiums on total and permanent disability
Receive a fixed daily benefit during hospitalisation
detail.explanation: The GIO rider is not cover itself. It is a right to purchase additional insurance at specified dates or life events without providing evidence of insurability, priced at the age of exercise.

detail.question: An Accidental Death Benefit Rider that pays an amount equal to the basic sum assured on accidental death is commonly referred to as providing:
detail.explanation: Because the additional payout equals the basic sum assured, it is called double indemnity. It can be three or more times, but the equal-amount case gives the term. The accident must meet the external, violent and visible means definition.

detail.question: Which situation involves a PURE risk?
options: Placing a bet on a football match
A driver colliding with another car
detail.explanation: A car collision has only loss or no loss, no chance of gain: pure risk, insurable. Betting, product launches and currency trading all carry a possibility of gain, so they are speculative.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, pure vs speculative risk

detail.question: Which is NOT one of the ways insurers combat anti-selection?
options: Guaranteeing acceptance with no health questions
detail.explanation: Guaranteed acceptance INVITES anti-selection, it doesn't combat it. Underwriting, excesses and pre-existing exclusions are the three defences.

detail.question: A key difference between insurance and gambling is that:
options: Insurance creates a new risk; gambling transfers an existing one
Insurance transfers an existing pure risk; gambling creates a new speculative one
detail.explanation: Insurance handles a pre-existing pure risk and restores the insured; gambling manufactures a new speculative risk and is socially unproductive.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurance vs gambling

detail.question: Under utmost good faith, which fact must a proposer disclose?
options: A fact that reduces the risk
A previous serious illness that increases the risk
A fact the insurer already knows
detail.explanation: Material facts that increase the risk must be disclosed. Facts of law, risk-reducing facts, and facts the insurer already knows are exempt from disclosure.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, utmost good faith

detail.question: Being significantly overweight, which raises the chance of a health claim, is an example of:
detail.explanation: A bodily condition raising the chance of loss is a physical hazard. Moral hazard is about dishonesty; anti-selection is about who chooses to buy.

detail.question: A consumer is unhappy with how a claim was handled and mediation has failed. The next step within FIDReC is:
options: Referral to MAS for a ruling
detail.explanation: FIDReC's process is mediation first, then adjudication if unresolved. MAS regulates but does not adjudicate individual disputes; SDIC handles insurer insolvency.

detail.question: The principle of indemnity means an insured:
options: Can profit from a covered loss
Is restored to their financial position before the loss, no more
Must always accept a cash settlement
Loses the right to sue a negligent third party
detail.explanation: Indemnity restores the insured to their pre-loss position without profit. (Life policies are benefit policies, an exception to strict indemnity.)

detail.question: An insurer expects claims of S$800 per policy, S$70 of investment income, and needs S$140 of expenses and profit. The net premium is:
detail.explanation: Net = claims minus investment income = 800 - 70 = S$730. (Gross would then be 730 + 140 = S$870, the tempting wrong answer.)

detail.question: CPF savings may be used to buy which premium type(s)?
options: Single premium and recurrent single premium
detail.explanation: CPF monies can buy only single premium or recurrent single premium policies.

detail.question: Which statement about a SINGLE premium policy is correct?
options: It is paid in yearly instalments and can lapse
The entire premium is paid once, and it is cheaper than the sum of equivalent regular premiums
It is only available for term insurance
detail.explanation: Single premium: one payment, cheaper overall because the insurer invests it all upfront. Recurring-but-never-lapsing describes recurrent single premium, a different mode.

detail.question: All of the following usually reduce the premium rate EXCEPT:
detail.explanation: Monthly payment costs about 2% MORE than annual, so it raises, not reduces, the effective premium. Non-smoker status, larger sum assured and being female all reduce the rate.

detail.question: For HEALTH insurance, which statement about gender is correct?
options: Women always pay less than men
Women above 65 tend to pay more, reflecting higher hospitalisation
Gender is never used in health pricing
detail.explanation: The health flip: women above 65 tend to pay more due to higher hospitalisation rates, the reverse of the life-insurance pattern.

detail.question: Why do larger policies often get a discount on the premium rate per S$1,000 of cover?
options: Fixed administration costs are spread over more cover
detail.explanation: Admin costs are largely fixed regardless of size, so spreading them over a bigger sum lowers the per-S$1,000 cost. Nothing to do with mortality or health.

detail.question: Which policy type is NOT a statutory insurance fund category under the Insurance Act?
detail.explanation: The three statutory fund categories are participating, non-participating and investment-linked. 'Group' is an ownership arrangement, not a fund category.

detail.question: A Universal Life policy is:
options: A participating whole life paying guaranteed bonuses
A non-par, interest-sensitive plan with a declared crediting rate and flexible premiums
A term policy with a savings rider
Always sold on a group basis
detail.explanation: Universal Life is non-par, interest-sensitive, with a declared crediting rate (guaranteed minimum) and flexible premiums in amount, method and timing.

detail.question: A wealthy couple wants a policy paying out only on the death of the second spouse, to fund estate duties. This is:
detail.explanation: Payment on the second death, for estate planning, is a last survivor / second-to-die policy. First-to-die pays on the first death.

detail.question: Which is an advantage of a voluntary (contributory) group plan over a contractual one?
options: Automatic cover for all eligible employees
Employees share the cost, reducing the employer's outlay
detail.explanation: In a voluntary plan employees pay part of the cost. Low admin, auto-cover and no minimum-participation requirement are features of the contractual plan.

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT true of a third-party policy?
options: The policy owner and life insured are different people
The roles of owner and life insured are unequal
A payor benefit rider may waive premiums if the owner dies
The owner and life insured are always spouses insured jointly
detail.explanation: Two spouses insured jointly describes joint life, not third-party. Third-party = different owner and life insured with unequal roles, often with a payor benefit rider.

detail.question: Group insurance premiums are generally cheaper than individual cover mainly because:
options: Group underwriting and one master contract lower administration and selection costs
detail.explanation: One master contract plus group underwriting/experience rating cuts admin and selection costs, making group cover cheaper. Not a subsidy or an age effect.

detail.question: Whole Life and Endowment policies share which characteristic that Term does not?
options: They can be issued as participating policies
They must be kept in a separate fund
detail.explanation: Whole Life and Endowment can be participating; Term is non-par only. Separate funds apply to ILPs, and endowments also pay on survival.

detail.question: A homeowner wants cover that falls in step with a 20-year housing loan. The suitable term shape is:
detail.explanation: A shrinking loan needs shrinking cover: decreasing term (mortgage reducing term). Draw the need as a falling line, match the falling shape.

detail.question: Which statement about a mortgage decreasing term policy is correct?
options: The sum assured always equals the exact outstanding loan
The sum assured follows a schedule fixed at outset using an assumed interest rate
The premium rises each year with the loan
It pays a maturity value if the borrower survives
detail.explanation: The reducing schedule is fixed at outset on assumptions, so the payout tracks but need not exactly equal the actual loan. It is pure protection with no maturity value.

detail.question: Which non-forfeiture choice provides the FULL original sum assured, but only for a limited period, with no further premiums?
detail.explanation: Full sum, fixed period = extended term (tall-and-short). 'Extended TERM' contains term = fixed period. Reduced paid-up keeps lifetime cover but a smaller sum.

detail.question: A policyholder stops paying but makes no election. Under the automatic premium loan provision, the insurer will:
options: Immediately surrender the policy for cash
Advance the cash value as a loan (with interest) to pay the premiums due
Convert the policy to extended term at once
detail.explanation: APL lends against the cash value, with interest, to keep premiums paid until the value is exhausted. Extended term is the later fallback if nothing is elected and the value runs out.

detail.question: 'Whole Life' in the product name refers to:
options: The period over which premiums are paid
The duration of the insurance protection
The length of the free-look period
The time until the policy matures at 65
detail.explanation: 'Whole Life' describes lifelong PROTECTION, not the premium-paying period. Ordinary vs limited-payment whole life differ only in how long you pay.

detail.question: A Limited Premium Payment Whole Life policy 'paid-up at 65' means:
options: Premiums stop at 65 but cover continues for life
The maturity value is paid at 65
The policy converts to term at 65
detail.explanation: Paid-up at 65 = premiums cease at 65; lifelong cover continues. Don't confuse premium term with coverage term.

detail.question: Which need is best matched to an endowment policy?
options: Providing a lump sum for a child's university fees in 15 years
Cheapest possible protection for a fixed term
detail.explanation: A dated savings goal with protection meanwhile = endowment. Decreasing loan = decreasing term; cheapest fixed-term = term; lifelong = whole life.

detail.question: Among term, whole life and endowment, which typically has the HIGHEST premium for the same sum assured, and why?
options: Whole life, because it lasts a lifetime
Endowment, because a payout is almost certain (death or maturity)
detail.explanation: Endowment is dearest because someone is paid in every outcome, so the insurer will almost certainly pay. Term is cheapest (may pay nothing); whole life sits between.

detail.question: Which document originates from the LIA rather than a MAS notice?
detail.explanation: 'Your Guide To Life Insurance' comes from the LIA. The Product Summary, Policy Illustration and Product Highlights Sheet are tied to MAS notices (FAA-N16 / Notice 307).

detail.question: On an online no-advice platform, before purchase the client must acknowledge a self-assessment (Annex A) covering:
detail.explanation: The Annex A self-check covers affordability (via the MoneySense budget calculator), whether a Direct Purchase Insurance product would suit, and general suitability.

detail.question: A factory owner insures his warehouse against fire. Six months later he sells the warehouse but keeps the policy running. It burns down the next week. Why is his claim likely to fail?
options: He no longer has insurable interest at the time of loss
The policy lapsed automatically on sale
detail.explanation: General insurance requires insurable interest at BOTH inception and the time of loss. He had it at inception but lost it when he sold, so at the moment of the fire he had none.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurable interest timing (general)

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT a requirement of an insurable risk?
options: The loss must be measurable in money
There must be a large number of similar exposure units
The loss must offer the chance of financial gain
detail.explanation: A chance of gain makes a risk speculative and UNinsurable. Insurable risks are pure risks: measurable, numerous, fortuitous, not catastrophic to the insurer. Read 'NOT' carefully.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, requirements of insurable risk

detail.question: An agent tells a client a minor detail is unimportant and not to bother disclosing it. The client relies on this. The doctrine that may stop the insurer denying the claim later is:
detail.explanation: Waiver: the insurer, through its agent, gave up the right to that information, so it cannot later rely on non-disclosure of it. Indemnity/subrogation/contribution are unrelated claim-settlement principles.

detail.question: SDIC's policy owners' protection scheme is designed to:
options: Protect policy owners if an insurer fails, within scheme limits
detail.explanation: The Policy Owners' Protection Scheme (run by SDIC) protects policyholders if an insurer becomes insolvent, subject to caps. It doesn't guarantee returns or cover mis-selling.

detail.question: The likelihood that a policyholder will act dishonestly, such as staging a loss, is called:
detail.explanation: Moral hazard concerns dishonest conduct or intent. Physical hazard is a bodily/property characteristic; anti-selection is worse-than-average risks being keener to buy.

detail.question: Which pair correctly matches the market body to its role?
options: LIA regulates insurers; MAS is an industry association
MAS is the regulator; LIA is the life insurers' industry association
FIDReC sets premium rates; SDIC mediates disputes
GIA regulates life insurance; LIA regulates general insurance
detail.explanation: MAS is the statutory regulator; LIA is the Life Insurance Association (industry body). FIDReC resolves disputes, SDIC runs protection schemes, GIA is the general insurance association.

detail.question: A man buys a life policy on his business partner, on whom the business depends financially. At what point must insurable interest exist?
options: At the time of the partner's death only
Insurable interest is not required between business partners
detail.explanation: Life insurance requires insurable interest at inception only. A business partner with financial dependency is a recognised insurable-interest relationship.

detail.question: The loading added to a net premium covers all EXCEPT:
options: An allowance for policies that lapse early
The mortality cost of the risk
detail.explanation: Mortality cost is part of the NET premium, not the loading. Loading covers expenses, commission, lapse allowance and profit. This is your net-vs-gross distinction as a NOT-question.

detail.question: A policy owner wants to pay for his whole life plan over just 15 years instead of for life, then stop. Compared with paying for life, each premium will be:
options: Lower, because fewer years of cover
Higher, because the same cost is compressed into fewer payments
The same, because cover is identical
Zero after year 15 with no effect on cost
detail.explanation: Limited payment compresses the same lifetime cost into fewer instalments, so each premium is higher. Cover still runs for life.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 2, payment modes / Ch 4 limited payment

detail.question: Which premium mode consists of a series of stand-alone single premiums, where stopping at any time leaves the policy fully paid-up rather than lapsed?
detail.explanation: Recurrent single premium: each payment is its own single premium buying a paid-up chunk, so stopping never causes a lapse. A single premium is ONE payment only; regular premium lapses if unpaid.

detail.question: Which factor would NOT normally affect the premium an insurer charges for a life policy?
options: The applicant's occupation as a librarian versus an accountant, both low-risk
detail.explanation: Between two equally low-risk desk occupations, occupation makes no rating difference. Smoking, sum assured and payment frequency are all listed pricing factors.

detail.question: An insurer assumes a 4% investment return when pricing. Actual returns come in at 2%. Who bears the shortfall on a guaranteed non-par policy?
options: The policyholder, via a higher premium next year
detail.explanation: Premiums are priced on the assumed rate; the insurer carries the gap if actual returns fall short on guaranteed non-par business. Non-par has no bonus to cut.

detail.question: For a whole life policy, a woman aged 30 is typically charged a premium based on the mortality of:
detail.explanation: Women live longer, so 'reduction in age' prices a woman as a younger man for life cover. (Health insurance flips this above 65.)

detail.question: Which statement about statutory insurance funds is correct?
options: All three fund types must be kept separate from each other
Participating and non-participating may share a fund; investment-linked must be separate
Investment-linked may share with participating; non-par must be separate
Funds may be freely combined at the insurer's discretion
detail.explanation: Par and non-par MAY share a fund; ILP MUST be separate. The must-versus-may line is the trap.

detail.question: Which of the following can NOT be issued as a participating policy?
detail.explanation: Term (and Universal Life) are non-par only, with no bonus-sharing surplus. Whole Life, Endowment and anticipated endowment can be participating.

detail.question: A married couple wants one policy that pays on the FIRST death, to clear their joint housing loan. This is a:
detail.explanation: Two lives, pays on the first death, cover then ends: first-to-die joint life, the classic joint-mortgage structure. Last survivor pays on the second death.

detail.question: Under a non-contributory (contractual) group scheme:
options: Employees pay part of the premium and may opt out
The employer pays all premiums and all eligible employees are automatically covered
The insurer requires a minimum participation rate
detail.explanation: Non-contributory = employer pays everything, everyone eligible is auto-covered. Cost-sharing, opting out and minimum-participation belong to the voluntary/contributory plan.

detail.question: Which is NOT a typical feature of group insurance compared with individual insurance?
options: One master contract covers many lives
Cover generally ceases when a member leaves the group
Each member selects their own sum assured and is individually underwritten
It is usually cheaper than equivalent individual cover
detail.explanation: Group insurance uses group underwriting and employer-set cover, not individual selection and underwriting. The other three are genuine group features.

detail.question: A grandmother takes out and owns an education endowment on her grandchild's life. This ownership type is:
detail.explanation: Owner (grandmother) differs from the life insured (grandchild) with unequal roles: third-party. Single life would need owner and life insured to be the same person.

detail.question: Since 1 October 2001, when selling group insurance products, a representative must:
options: Obtain MAS pre-approval for each policy
Offer a free-look period of 60 days
detail.explanation: Since 1 Oct 2001 reps must complete a fact-find when selling group products. The other options are unrelated requirements.

detail.question: A company insures a key executive whose value to the firm is expected to stay level for 10 years. Best policy and beneficiary:
detail.explanation: Level need = level term; the company suffers the financial loss so it is the beneficiary. Key-person insurance is level term with the company as beneficiary.

detail.question: Which statement about the renewable option on a term policy is FALSE?
options: Renewal requires no evidence of insurability
The premium rises to reflect attained age
The sum assured is reduced at each renewal
Renewals are usually allowed only up to about age 60
detail.explanation: The sum assured is NOT reduced; renewal keeps the same benefit and term, only the premium rises. The other three are true. This is a NOT/FALSE question, mark each one.

detail.question: Mdm Lee, 60, stops paying her whole life premiums. She wants cover to continue for the rest of her life with no further payments, accepting a smaller sum. The right non-forfeiture option is:
detail.explanation: Lifelong cover, no further premiums, reduced amount = reduced paid-up (long-and-low). 'Lifelong' rules out extended term, which is a fixed period.

detail.question: A policyholder wants to keep his FULL sum assured after stopping premiums, and doesn't mind that cover will run only for a fixed number of years. He should choose:
detail.explanation: Full sum for a fixed period = extended term (tall-and-short). Reduced paid-up keeps lifetime cover but shrinks the sum. This is the mirror image of the reduced paid-up question.

detail.question: Which is NOT a condition of converting a term policy to a permanent one?
options: A written request with the premium
New sum assured not exceeding the original
Original policy exclusions continue to apply
detail.explanation: Evidence of insurability is NOT required, that waiver is the point of the option. The other three are genuine conditions.

detail.question: A whole life policy accumulates a cash value primarily because:
options: The insurer adds an annual savings bonus
Level premiums exceed the true cost of risk in the early years, and the excess is invested
Policyholders pay a separate investment premium
MAS mandates a minimum cash value
detail.explanation: Level premium on a rising risk means early overpayment, which is banked and invested: that is the cash value. It's structural, not an added feature.

detail.question: An endowment policy is best suited to a client who wants:
options: A lump sum at a specific future date, plus protection until then
Lifelong cover with no savings element
Cover that decreases with a loan
detail.explanation: Endowment matches a dated goal (education, retirement target) with protection meanwhile: maturity value on survival, sum assured on earlier death. Cheapest death-only = term; lifelong = whole life; loan-linked = decreasing term.

detail.question: Mr Tan's anticipated endowment (sum assured S$40,000) has paid him S$12,000 in cashbacks. He dies before maturity. His beneficiaries receive:
detail.explanation: Full S$40,000 sum assured. Cashbacks already received are not deducted from the death benefit.

detail.question: When recommending a plain participating whole life policy (not bundled, not an ILP), which document is NOT required?
detail.explanation: The Product Highlights Sheet is required only for ILPs. A plain par whole life needs the Cover Page, Product Summary and Policy Illustration (plus the LIA Guide).

detail.question: On an online no-advice platform, a term policy is issued with a pre-existing-condition exclusion added at underwriting. Before the client can buy, the platform must:
options: Highlight the conditional acceptance and obtain the client's acknowledgement
Refer the client to a human adviser
detail.explanation: Conditional acceptance (exclusions or loadings from underwriting) must be highlighted and acknowledged before purchase on a no-advice platform.

detail.question: Which of the following is a PURE risk that an insurer would be willing to cover?
options: Investing S$10,000 in a friend's start-up
The family home being destroyed by fire
Opening a hawker stall hoping to profit
detail.explanation: Fire destroying a home has only two outcomes, loss or no loss, so it is a pure risk and insurable. The other three carry a possibility of gain, making them speculative and uninsurable.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, pure vs speculative risk

detail.question: A 45-year-old applicant has a history of two heart attacks. In underwriting terms, this is:
detail.explanation: A health or body characteristic that raises the chance of loss is a physical hazard. Moral hazard concerns dishonesty or suspicious motive, not medical history.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, physical vs moral hazard

detail.question: For a life insurance policy, insurable interest must exist:
options: At inception and at the time of the claim
At the time of the claim only
detail.explanation: Life needs insurable interest at inception only. It is general (property) insurance that needs it at both inception and the time of loss. Keep the two rules side by side.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurable interest timing

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT a recognised exemption from the duty of disclosure under utmost good faith?
options: Facts the insurer could reasonably be expected to know
detail.explanation: Facts that worsen the risk are exactly what must be disclosed, so they are not an exemption. Facts of law, facts the insurer is presumed to know, and facts that reduce the risk are among the recognised exemptions.

detail.question: The principle that a person may not recover more than their actual financial loss is:
detail.explanation: Indemnity restores the insured to their pre-loss financial position, with no profit from a claim. Life policies are technically benefit policies rather than strict indemnity.

detail.question: Which statement about insurance versus gambling is correct?
options: Both create a new speculative risk
Insurance handles an existing pure risk; gambling creates a new speculative risk
Gambling handles an existing pure risk; insurance creates a new speculative risk
detail.explanation: Insurance handles an existing pure risk and is socially productive; gambling manufactures a new speculative risk and is socially unproductive. Mock Exam 2 Q15 swaps these words.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, insurance vs gambling

detail.question: Which body handles disputes between consumers and financial institutions in Singapore, offering adjudication after mediation?
detail.explanation: FIDReC resolves consumer disputes through mediation then adjudication. MAS is the regulator, SDIC runs deposit and policy protection, and LIA is the industry association.
detail.reference: M9 e-textbook Ch 1, Singapore market / FIDReC

detail.question: An insurer expects claims of S$600 per policyholder, expects S$50 of investment income per policy, and needs S$120 for expenses and profit. The GROSS premium is:
detail.explanation: Net = 600 - 50 = S$550. Gross = net + loading = 550 + 120 = S$670. S$720 forgets the investment-income discount; S$550 stops at the net premium.

detail.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven factors used in pricing a life premium?
detail.explanation: Nationality is not a pricing factor, a common distractor like occupation. Smoking, frequency of payment and sum assured are all on the list of seven.

detail.question: Paying premiums monthly rather than annually usually:
detail.explanation: Monthly payment costs roughly 2% more than annual, because the insurer loses some investment income and incurs more admin from spreading collection.

detail.question: A 24-year-old female is charged the same rate as a 21-year-old male. This pricing method is called:
detail.explanation: Reduction in age reflects that females live longer, so a female is priced as a younger male for life cover.

detail.question: Which premium mode requires the whole premium to be paid once, and is cheaper than the sum of equivalent regular premiums?
detail.explanation: Single premium is one lump sum at the outset, cheaper overall because the insurer gets all the money upfront to invest.

detail.question: Larger sum assured policies often attract a discount on the rate per S$1,000 because:
options: Administration costs are largely fixed regardless of policy size
Larger policies must be single premium
detail.explanation: Fixed admin cost spread over a bigger policy means a lower cost per S$1,000. It has nothing to do with mortality.

detail.question: Under the Insurance Act 1966, which type of policy MUST be maintained in its own separate insurance fund?
detail.explanation: Investment-linked policies must be kept in a separate fund. Par and non-par may share one. The must-versus-may distinction is the classic trap (Set B Q66).

detail.question: Which product is offered ONLY on a non-participating basis?
detail.explanation: Universal Life, along with Term, is non-par only. Whole Life, Endowment and the anticipated endowment variant can be participating.

detail.question: A Universal Life policy is best described as:
options: A participating whole life with guaranteed bonuses
An interest-sensitive non-par plan with flexible premiums and a declared crediting rate
A term policy with a maturity value
A group plan for high-net-worth clients
detail.explanation: Universal Life is non-par, interest sensitive, with a declared crediting rate (subject to a guaranteed minimum) and premiums flexible in amount, method and timing.

detail.question: An estate-planning couple wants a policy that pays out only when the SECOND of them dies. This is a:
detail.explanation: Paying on the second death is a last survivor or second-to-die policy, typically used for estate planning and uncommon in Singapore. First-to-die pays on the first death.

detail.question: Under a voluntary (contributory) group plan, the insurer typically requires:
options: The employer to pay all premiums
A minimum level of employee participation
Every eligible employee to be automatically covered
detail.explanation: Because employees opt in and pay part of the cost, the insurer demands minimum participation to defend against anti-selection.

detail.question: Which of the following is an advantage of a CONTRACTUAL (non-contributory) group plan?
options: Employees choose their own level of cover
Employees share part of the premium cost
Ease of administration, lower cost, and employer control over the benefit structure
Employees may opt in or out at any time
detail.explanation: Easy admin, lower cost and employer control are the contractual advantages. The other three describe the voluntary plan.

detail.question: Compared with individual insurance, group insurance is generally characterised by:
options: A separate contract for each employee
One master contract with group underwriting or experience rating
Cover that continues after the member leaves the group
detail.explanation: Group insurance uses one master contract, group underwriting or experience rating, is cheaper, and cover ends when you leave. The other options describe individual insurance.

detail.question: A third-party policy is one where:
options: The policy owner and the life insured are the same person
The policy owner and the life insured are different people, with unequal roles
Two lives are insured under one contract
One master contract covers many lives
detail.explanation: Third-party means owner and life insured are different people with unequal roles, for example a father owning a policy on his child. Owner equalling life insured is a single life policy.

detail.question: A borrower wants cover on a S$250,000 renovation loan being repaid over 6 years. The most suitable term shape is:
detail.explanation: A loan balance shrinks over time, so the cover should shrink too: decreasing term, in the Credit Life family. Sketch the need as a line: falling need, falling cover.

detail.question: Converting a term policy to whole life requires the client to:
options: Prove they are still in good health
Do neither of the above; conversion needs no health evidence
detail.explanation: Conversion is done without evidence of insurability, so no health proof is needed. That guarantee is the whole value of the convertible option (Mock 1 Q63).

detail.question: Whole life insurance builds a cash value because:
options: The insurer adds a savings bonus each year
Level premiums overcharge in early years relative to rising mortality, and the excess is banked
Policyholders pay a separate savings premium on top
The government subsidises whole life policies
detail.explanation: A level premium on a rising risk means you overpay when young; that banked excess, invested, is the cash value. It is a structural by-product, not an add-on.

detail.question: A policyholder stops paying and elects nothing. The insurer advances the cash value as a loan to keep paying the premiums. This is the:
detail.explanation: The Automatic Premium Loan lends against the cash value, with interest, to pay premiums until the value runs out.

detail.question: Which non-forfeiture option keeps the policy providing lifelong protection at a reduced sum assured, with no further premiums?
detail.explanation: Reduced paid-up whole life keeps lifetime cover at a smaller sum with no further premiums. Extended term keeps the full sum but only for a fixed period; surrender ends cover.

detail.question: A Limited Premium Payment Whole Life policy is paid-up at age 65. This means:
options: Premiums stop at 65 but cover continues for the whole of life
The policy converts to term at 65
The sum assured is paid out at 65
detail.explanation: Paid-up at 65 means premiums stop at 65; cover continues for the whole of life. Do not confuse premium term with coverage term.

detail.question: An endowment policy differs from whole life mainly because it:
options: Pays a maturity value if the insured survives the term
Can only be bought on a non-par basis
detail.explanation: Endowment adds a survival payout: the sum assured on death during the term, or the maturity value if alive at the end. That survival certainty is why it is the priciest of the three.

detail.question: Mr Goh's anticipated endowment has a sum assured of S$60,000. He has received S$18,000 in cashbacks and then dies before maturity. His beneficiaries receive:
detail.explanation: The full S$60,000 sum assured is paid. Cashback slices already received are not deducted from the death benefit.

detail.question: Which document must a representative furnish ONLY when recommending an Investment-Linked Policy?
detail.explanation: The Product Highlights Sheet is required specifically for ILPs. The Product Summary, Policy Illustration and Cover Page apply across life policies generally.
